This time, we observe a considerable out-performance of \emph{a03} against \emph{a02} on criterion g02 (see second row in the relation table above). We therefore notice a positively polarized \emph{certainly confirmed} outranking situation in this case \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id15}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2013]{\emph{{[}BIS-2013{]}}}}.


\subsubsection{Recoding the valuation}
\label{tutorial:recoding-the-valuation}\begin{description}
\item[{All outranking digraphs, being of root type {\hyperref[techDoc:digraphs.Digraph]{\emph{\code{digraphs.Digraph}}}}, inherit the methods available under this class. The characteristic valuation domain of an outranking digraph may be recoded with the \code{digraphs.Digraph.recodeValutaion()} method below to the integer range {[}-37,+37{]}, i.e. plus or minus the global significance of the family of criteria considered in this example instance:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{odg}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{recodeValuation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{37}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+m+mi}{37}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{odg}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{valuationdomain}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{hasIntegerValuation}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n+nb+bp}{True}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{Digraph}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRelationTable}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{odg}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{* \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Relation Table \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{* \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Relation Table \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{  S   \textbar{} \PYGZsq{}a01\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a02\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a03\PYGZsq{}  \PYGZsq{}a04\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a05\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a06\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a07\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\textbar{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a01\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}    0     +11     \PYGZhy{}11     +5     +17     +14     +17}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a02\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +5       0     \PYGZhy{}37    +13      +5     +15     \PYGZhy{}14}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a03\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}  +31     +37       0    +34     +31     +31     +26}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a04\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +9     +18     \PYGZhy{}21      0      +9     +19      +9}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a05\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}  +19     +37     \PYGZhy{}26    +27       0     +19     +12}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a06\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +6     +27     \PYGZhy{}19    +13     +12       0     +14}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a07\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}  +25     +17      \PYGZhy{}9     +9      +9     +17       0}
\PYG{g+go}{Valuation domain:  \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}hasIntegerValuation\PYGZsq{}: True, \PYGZsq{}min\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}\PYGZhy{}37\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{                    \PYGZsq{}max\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}37\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}med\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}0.000\PYGZsq{})\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}
Notice that the reflexive self comparison characteristic \(r(x S x)\) is set by default to the median indeterminate valuation value 0; the reflexive terms of binary relation being generally ignored in most of the \code{Digraph3} resources.
\end{notice}


\subsubsection{Codual digraph}
\label{tutorial:codual-digraph}\begin{description}
\item[{From the theory (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id16}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2013]{\emph{{[}BIS-2013{]}}}}, \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id17}{\hyperref[tutorial:adt-l7]{\emph{{[}ADT-L7{]}}}} )  we know that the bipolarly outranking relation is \textbf{weakly complete}, i.e. if \(r(x\,S\,y) < 0.0\) then \(r(y\,S\,x) >= 0.0\) . From this property follows that the bipolarly valued outranking relation verifies the coduality principle: the dual (-) of the converse (\textasciitilde{}) of the outranking relation corresponds to its strict outranking part. We may visualize the codual (strict) outranking digraph with a graphviz drawing \footnotemark[1]:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cdodg} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{o}{\PYGZti{}}\PYG{n}{odg}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cdodg}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{codualOdg}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} exporting a dot file for GraphViz tools \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Exporting to codualOdg.dot}
\PYG{g+go}{dot \PYGZhy{}Grankdir=BT \PYGZhy{}Tpng codualOdg.dot \PYGZhy{}o codualOdg.png}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

{\hfill\includegraphics{codualOdg.png}\hfill}

It becomes readily clear now from the picture above that alternative \emph{a03} strictly outranks in fact all the other alternatives. Hence, \emph{a03} appears as \textbf{Condorcet winner} and may be recommended as \emph{best decision action} in this illustrative preference modelling exercise.


\subsubsection{XMCDA 2.0}
\label{tutorial:xmcda-2-0}\begin{description}
\item[{As with all Digraph instances, it is possible to store permanently a copy of the outranking digraph \emph{odg}. As its outranking relation is automatically generated by the {\hyperref[techDoc:outrankingDigraphs.BipolarOutrankingDigraph]{\emph{\code{outrankingDigraphs.BipolarOutrankingDigraph}}}} class constructor on the basis of a given performance tableau, it is sufficient to save only the latter. For this purpose we are using the \href{http://www.decision-deck.org/xmcda/}{XMCDA 2.00} XML encoding scheme of MCDA data, as provided by the Decision Deck Project (see \href{http://www.decision-deck.org/}{http://www.decision-deck.org/}):}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{PerformanceTableau}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{saveXMCDA2}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{odg}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{tutorialPerfTab}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} saving performance tableau in XMCDA 2.0 format  \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{File: tutorialPerfTab.xml saved !}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}
\end{Verbatim}

\item[{The resulting XML file my be visualized in a browser window (other than Chrome or Chromium)  with a corresponding XMCDA style sheet (see here). Hitting \code{Ctrl U} in Firefox will open a browser window showing the underlying xml encoded raw text. It is thus possible to easily edit and update as needed a given performance tableau instance. Reinstantiating again a corresponding updated \emph{odg} object goes like follow:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{pt} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{XMCDA2PerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{tutorialPerfTab}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{odg} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{pt}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{odg}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRelationTable}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{* \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Relation Table \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{  S   \textbar{}  \PYGZsq{}a01\PYGZsq{}     \PYGZsq{}a02\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a03\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a04\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a05\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a06\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a07\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\textbar{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a01\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +0.00   +29.73  \PYGZhy{}29.73  +13.51  +48.65  +40.54  +48.65}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a02\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +13.51  +0.00  \PYGZhy{}100.00  +37.84  +13.51  +43.24  \PYGZhy{}37.84}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a03\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +83.78  +100.00  +0.00  +91.89  +83.78  +83.78  +70.27}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a04\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +24.32  +48.65  \PYGZhy{}56.76   +0.00  +24.32  +51.35  +24.32}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a05\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +51.35  +100.00  \PYGZhy{}70.27  +72.97  +0.00  +51.35  +32.43}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a06\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +16.22  +72.97  \PYGZhy{}51.35  +35.14  +32.43   +0.00  +37.84}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a07\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +67.57  +45.95  \PYGZhy{}24.32  +27.03  +27.03  +45.95   +0.00}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

We recover the original bipolarly valued outranking characteristics, and we may restart again the preference modelling process.

Many more tools for exploiting bipolarly valued outranking digraphs are available in the Digraph3 resources (see the technical documentation of the {\hyperref[techDoc:outrankingdigraphs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{outrankingDigraphs module}}}} and the {\hyperref[techDoc:perftabs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{perfTabs module}}}}).

Back to {\hyperref[tutorial:tutorial-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{Tutorials of the Digraph3 resources}}}}


\subsection{Generating random performance tableaux}
\label{tutorial:generating-random-performance-tableaux}\label{tutorial:randomperformacetableau-tutorial-label}\setbox0\vbox{
\begin{minipage}{0.95\linewidth}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\phantomsection\label{tutorial:id78}{\hyperref[tutorial:the-randomperftabs-module]{\emph{The randomPerfTabs module}}}

\item {} 
\phantomsection\label{tutorial:id79}{\hyperref[tutorial:the-randomperformancetableau-generator]{\emph{The RandomPerformanceTableau generator}}}

\item {} 
\phantomsection\label{tutorial:id80}{\hyperref[tutorial:the-randomrankperformancetableau-generator]{\emph{The RandomRankPerformanceTableau generator}}}

\item {} 
\phantomsection\label{tutorial:id81}{\hyperref[tutorial:the-randomcbperformancetableau-generator]{\emph{The RandomCBPerformanceTableau generator}}}

\item {} 
\phantomsection\label{tutorial:id82}{\hyperref[tutorial:the-random3objectivesperformancetableau-generator]{\emph{The Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau generator}}}

\end{itemize}
\end{minipage}}
\begin{center}\setlength{\fboxsep}{5pt}\shadowbox{\box0}\end{center}


\subsubsection{The randomPerfTabs module}
\label{tutorial:the-randomperftabs-module}
This module provides several random performance tableaux generators, i.e. PerformanceTableau class instances (see the perfTabs module), mainly for the purpose of testing implemented versions of methods and tools presented and discussed in the Algorithmic Decision Theory course at the University of Luxembourg. This tutorial concerns the four most useful generators:
\begin{enumerate}
\item {} 
The simplest model, called \textbf{RandomPerformaceTableau}, generates
a set of \emph{n} decision actions, a set of \emph{m} real-valued
performance criteria, ranging by default from 0.0 to 100.0,
associated with default discrimination thresholds: 10.0 (ind.), 20.0 (pref.) and 80.0 (veto). The generated performances are uniformly distributed on each measurement scale.

\item {} 
In order to study aggregation of linear orders, we provide a model called \textbf{RandomRankPerformanceTableau} which provides linearly ordered performances without ties on multiple criteria for a given number of decision actions.

\item {} 
One of the most useful random generator, called
\textbf{RandomCBPerformanceTableau}, proposes two decision objectives,
named \emph{Costs} (to be minimized) respectively \emph{Benefits} (to be
maximized) model; its purpose being to generate more or less
contradictory performances on these two, usually opposed,
objectives. \emph{Low costs} will randomly be coupled with \emph{low
benefits}, whereas \emph{high costs} will randomly be coupled with high benefits.

\item {} 
Many multiple criteria decision problems concern three decision
objectives which take into account \emph{economical}, \emph{societal} as
well as \emph{environmental} aspects. For this type of performance
tableau model, we provide a specific generator, called \textbf{Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau}.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{The RandomPerformanceTableau generator}
\label{tutorial:the-randomperformancetableau-generator}\begin{description}
\item[{The \code{RandomPerformanceTableau generator}, the simplest of the kind, specializes the \code{PerformanceTableau} class and takes the following parameters:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
numberOfActions := nbr of decision actions.

\item {} 
numberOfCriteria := number performance criteria.

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{weightDistribution := `random' (default) \textbar{} `fixed' \textbar{} `equisignificant'.}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] If `random', weights are uniformly selected randomly
\item[] from the given weight scale;
\item[] If `fixed', the weightScale must provided a corresponding weights
\item[] distribution;
\item[] If `equisignificant', all criterion weights are put to unity.
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\item {} 
weightScale := {[}Min,Max{]} (default =(1,numberOfCriteria).

\item {} 
IntegerWeights := True (default) \textbar{} False (normalized to proportions of 1.0).

\item {} 
commonScale := {[}Min;Max{]}; common performance measuring scales (default = {[}0;100{]})

\item {} 
commonThresholds := {[}(q0,q1),(p0,p1),(v0,v1){]}; common indifference(q), preference (p) and considerable performance difference discrimination thresholds. For each threshold type \emph{x} in \emph{\{q,p,v\}}, the float x0 value represents a constant and the float x1 value a proportional value. Default values are {[}(10.0,0.0),(20.0,0.0),(80.0,0,0){]}.

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{commonMode := common random distribution of random performance measurements:}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] (`uniform',Min,Max), uniformly distributed float values on the given common scales' range.
\item[] (`normal',mu,sigma), truncated Gaussian distribution.
\item[] (`triangular',mode,repartition), generalized triangular distribution with a probability repartition parameter specifying the probability mass accumulated until the mode value.
\item[] (`beta',alpha,beta), a beta generator with standard alpha and beta parameters.
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\item {} 
valueDigits := \textless{}integer\textgreater{}, precision of performance measurements (2 decimal digits by default).

\end{itemize}

\item[{Code example:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{randomPerfTabs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{RandomPerformanceTableau}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RandomPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{numberOfActions}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{numberOfCriteria}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{seed}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{actions}
\PYG{g+go}{    \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}comment\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}RandomPerformanceTableau() generated.\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}random decision action\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{},}
\PYG{g+go}{     \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}comment\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}RandomPerformanceTableau() generated.\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}random decision action\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{},}
\PYG{g+go}{     \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}comment\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}RandomPerformanceTableau() generated.\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}random decision action\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{criteria}
\PYG{g+go}{    \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}g1\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}thresholds\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}ind\PYGZsq{} : (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}10.0\PYGZsq{}), Decimal(\PYGZsq{}0.0\PYGZsq{})),}
\PYG{g+go}{                           \PYGZsq{}veto\PYGZsq{}: (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}80.0\PYGZsq{}), Decimal(\PYGZsq{}0.0\PYGZsq{})),}
\PYG{g+go}{                           \PYGZsq{}pref\PYGZsq{}: (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}20.0\PYGZsq{}), Decimal(\PYGZsq{}0.0\PYGZsq{}))\PYGZcb{},}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}scale\PYGZsq{}: [0.0, 100.0],}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}weight\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}digraphs.RandomPerformanceTableau() instance\PYGZsq{},}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}comment\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}Arguments: ; weightDistribution=random;}
\PYG{g+go}{                weightScale=(1, 1); commonMode=None\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{evaluation}
\PYG{g+go}{    \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}g01\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}a01\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}45.95\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{             \PYGZsq{}a02\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}95.17\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{             \PYGZsq{}a03\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}17.47\PYGZsq{})}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{    \PYGZcb{}}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}


\subsubsection{The RandomRankPerformanceTableau generator}
\label{tutorial:the-randomrankperformancetableau-generator}
Random generator for multiple criteria ranked (without ties) performances of a
given number of decision actions. On each criterion,
all decision actions are hence linearly ordered. The \code{RandomRankPerformanceTableau} class is
matching the \code{RandomLinearVotingProfile} class provided by  the votingDigraphs module.
\begin{description}
\item[{\emph{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
number of actions,

\item {} 
number of performance criteria,

\item {} 
weightDistribution := equisignificant \textbar{} random (default, see above above)

\item {} 
weightScale := (1, 1 \textbar{} numberOfCriteria (default when random))

\item {} 
integerWeights := Boolean (True = default)

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{commonThresholds (default) := \{}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] `ind':(0,0),
\item[] `pref':(1,0),
\item[] `veto':(numberOfActions,0)
\item[] \} (default)
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\end{itemize}

\end{description}


\subsubsection{The RandomCBPerformanceTableau generator}
\label{tutorial:the-randomcbperformancetableau-generator}
Random generation of \emph{Cost} versus \emph{Benefit} oriented performance tableaux follows the directives below:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
We distinguish three types of decision actions: \emph{cheap}, \emph{neutral} and \emph{expensive} ones with an equal proportion of 1/3. We also distinguish two types of weighted criteria: \emph{cost} criteria to be \emph{minimized}, and \emph{benefit} criteria to be \emph{maximized}; in the proportions 1/3 respectively 2/3.

\item {} 
Random performances on each type of criteria  are drawn, either from an ordinal scale {[}0;10{]}, or from a cardinal scale {[}0.0;100.0{]}, following a parametric triangular law of mode: 30\% performance for cheap, 50\% for neutral, and\$70\% performance for expensive decision actions, with constant probability repartition 0.5 on each side of the respective mode.

\item {} 
Cost criteria use mostly cardinal scales (3/4), whereas benefit criteria use mostly ordinal scales (2/3).

\item {} 
The sum of weights of the cost criteria by default equals the sum weights of the benefit criteria: weighDistribution = `equiobjectives'.

\item {} 
On cardinal criteria, both of cost or of benefit type, we observe following constant preference discrimination quantiles: 5\% indifferent situations, 90\% strict preference situations, and 5\% veto situation.

\end{itemize}
\begin{description}
\item[{\emph{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
If \emph{numberOfActions} == None, a uniform random number between 10 and 31 of cheap, neutral or advantageous actions (equal 1/3 probability each type) actions is instantiated

\item {} 
If \emph{numberOfCriteria} == None, a uniform random number between 5 and 21 of cost or benefit criteria (1/3 respectively 2/3 probability) is instantiated

\item {} 
\emph{weightDistribution} = \{`equiobjectives'\textbar{}'fixed'\textbar{}'random'\textbar{}'equisignificant' (default = `equisignificant')\}

\item {} 
default \emph{weightScale} for `random' weightDistribution is 1 - numberOfCriteria

\item {} 
All cardinal criteria are evaluated with decimals between 0.0 and 100.0 whereas ordinal criteria are evaluated with integers between 0 and 10.

\item {} 
commonThresholds is obsolete. Preference discrimination is specified as percentiles of concerned performance differences (see below).

\item {} 
commonPercentiles = \{`ind':5, `pref':10, {[}'weakveto':90,{]} `veto':95\} are expressed in percents (reversed for vetoes) and only concern cardinal criteria.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

\begin{notice}{warning}{Warning:}
Minimal number of decision actions required is 3 !
\end{notice}
\begin{description}
\item[{\textbf{Example Python session}:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{randomPerfTabs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{RandomCBPerformanceTableau}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RandomCBPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }         \PYG{n}{numberOfActions}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{7}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }         \PYG{n}{numberOfCriteria}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }         \PYG{n}{weightDistribution}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{equiobjectives}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }         \PYG{n}{commonPercentiles}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{ind}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{pref}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{l+m+mi}{10}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{veto}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{l+m+mi}{95}\PYG{p}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }         \PYG{n}{seed}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showActions}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} show decision action \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  a1}
\PYG{g+go}{  short name: a1}
\PYG{g+go}{  name:       random cheap decision action}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  a2}
\PYG{g+go}{  short name: a2}
\PYG{g+go}{  name:       random neutral decision action}
\PYG{g+gp}{...}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  a7}
\PYG{g+go}{  short name: a7}
\PYG{g+go}{  name:       random advantageous decision action}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showCriteria}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}  criteria \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{g1 \PYGZsq{}random ordinal benefit criterion\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{  Scale = (0, 10)}
\PYG{g+go}{  Weight = 0.167}
\PYG{g+go}{g2 \PYGZsq{}random cardinal cost criterion\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{  Scale = (0.0, 100.0)}
\PYG{g+go}{  Weight = 0.250}
\PYG{g+go}{  Threshold ind  :  1.76 + 0.00x ; percentile:  0.095}
\PYG{g+go}{  Threshold pref :  2.16 + 0.00x ; percentile:  0.143}
\PYG{g+go}{  Threshold veto : 73.19 + 0.00x ; percentile:  0.952}
\PYG{g+gp}{...}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

In this example we notice the three types of decision actions, as well as two types of criteria with either an ordinal or a cardinal performance measuring scale. In the latter case, by default about 5\% of the random performance differences will be below the \emph{indifference} and 10\% below the \emph{preference} discriminating threshold. About 5\% will be considered as \emph{considerably large}. More statistics about the generated performances is available as follows:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showStatistics}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Performance tableau summary statistics \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Instance name      : randomCBperftab}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsh{}Actions           : 7}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsh{}Criteria          : 5}
\PYG{g+go}{*Statistics per Criterion*}
\PYG{g+go}{Criterion name       : g1}
\PYG{g+go}{  Criterion weight     : 2}
\PYG{g+go}{  criterion scale    : 0.00 \PYGZhy{} 10.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  mean evaluation    : 5.14}
\PYG{g+go}{  standard deviation : 2.64}
\PYG{g+go}{  maximal evaluation : 8.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  quantile Q3 (x\PYGZus{}75) : 8.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  median evaluation  : 6.50}
\PYG{g+go}{  quantile Q1 (x\PYGZus{}25) : 3.50}
\PYG{g+go}{  minimal evaluation : 1.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  mean absolute difference      : 2.94}
\PYG{g+go}{  standard difference deviation : 3.74}
\PYG{g+go}{Criterion name       : g2}
\PYG{g+go}{  Criterion weight     : 3}
\PYG{g+go}{  criterion scale    : \PYGZhy{}100.00 \PYGZhy{} 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  mean evaluation    : \PYGZhy{}49.32}
\PYG{g+go}{  standard deviation : 27.59}
\PYG{g+go}{  maximal evaluation : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  quantile Q3 (x\PYGZus{}75) : \PYGZhy{}27.51}
\PYG{g+go}{  median evaluation  : \PYGZhy{}35.98}
\PYG{g+go}{  quantile Q1 (x\PYGZus{}25) : \PYGZhy{}54.02}
\PYG{g+go}{  minimal evaluation : \PYGZhy{}91.87}
\PYG{g+go}{  mean absolute difference      : 28.72}
\PYG{g+go}{  standard difference deviation : 39.02}
\PYG{g+gp}{...}
\end{Verbatim}

A (potentially ranked) colored heat map with 5 color levels is also provided:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showHTMLPerformanceHeatmap}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{colorLevels}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{Ranked}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{False}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{Verbatim}

{\hfill\includegraphics{randomCBHeatmap.png}\hfill}
\begin{description}
\item[{Such a performance tableau may be stored and re-accessed in the XMCDA2 encoded format:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{saveXMCDA2}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{temp}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} saving performance tableau in XMCDA 2.0 format  \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{File: temp.xml saved !}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{perfTabs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{XMCDA2PerformanceTableau}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{XMCDA2PerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{temp}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{o}{.}
\end{Verbatim}

\item[{If needed for instance in an R session, a CSV version of the performance tableau may be created as follows:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{saveCSV}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{temp}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{* \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Storing performance tableau in CSV format in file temp.csv}
\PYG{g+go}{...\PYGZdl{} less temp.csv}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZdq{}actions\PYGZdq{},\PYGZdq{}g1\PYGZdq{},\PYGZdq{}g2\PYGZdq{},\PYGZdq{}g3\PYGZdq{},\PYGZdq{}g4\PYGZdq{},\PYGZdq{}g5\PYGZdq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZdq{}a1\PYGZdq{},1.00,\PYGZhy{}17.92,\PYGZhy{}33.99,26.68,3.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZdq{}a2\PYGZdq{},8.00,\PYGZhy{}30.71,\PYGZhy{}77.77,66.35,6.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZdq{}a3\PYGZdq{},8.00,\PYGZhy{}41.65,\PYGZhy{}69.84,53.43,8.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZdq{}a4\PYGZdq{},2.00,\PYGZhy{}39.49,\PYGZhy{}16.99,18.62,2.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZdq{}a5\PYGZdq{},6.00,\PYGZhy{}91.87,\PYGZhy{}74.85,83.09,7.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZdq{}a6\PYGZdq{},7.00,\PYGZhy{}32.47,\PYGZhy{}24.91,79.24,9.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZdq{}a7\PYGZdq{},4.00,\PYGZhy{}91.11,\PYGZhy{}7.44,48.22,7.00}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

Back to {\hyperref[tutorial:tutorial-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{Tutorials of the Digraph3 resources}}}}


\subsubsection{The Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau generator}
\label{tutorial:the-random3objectivesperformancetableau-generator}
The class provides a generator of random performance tableaux concerning three preferential decision objectives which take into account \emph{economical}, \emph{societal} as well as \emph{environmental} aspects.

Each decision action is qualified randomly as performing \emph{weak} (-), \emph{fair} (\textasciitilde{}) or \emph{good} (+) on each of the three objectives.
\begin{description}
\item[{Generator directives are the following:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
numberOfActions = 20 (default),

\item {} 
numberOfCriteria = 13 (default),

\item {} 
weightDistribution = `equiobjectives' (default) \textbar{} `random' \textbar{} `equisignificant',

\item {} 
weightScale = (1,numberOfCriteria): only used when random criterion weights are requested,

\item {} 
integerWeights = True (default): False gives normlized rational weights,

\item {} 
commonScale = (0.0,100.0),

\item {} 
commonThresholds = {[}(5.0,0.0),(10.0,0.0),(60.0,0.0){]}: Performance discrimination thresholds may be set for `ind', `pref' and `veto',

\item {} 
commonMode = {[}'triangular','variable',0.5{]}: random number generators of various other types (`uniform','beta') are available,

\item {} 
valueDigits = 2 (default): evaluations are encoded as Decimals,

\item {} 
missingProbability = 0.05 (default): random insertion of missing values with given probability,

\item {} 
seed= None.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}
If the mode of the \emph{triangular} distribution is set to `\emph{variable}`,
three modes at 0.3 (-), 0.5 (\textasciitilde{}), respectively 0.7 (+) of the common scale span are set at random for each coalition and action.
\end{notice}

\begin{notice}{warning}{Warning:}
Minimal number of decision actions required is 3 !
\end{notice}
\begin{description}
\item[{\textbf{Example Python session}:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{randomPerfTabs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}
\PYG{g+go}{          numberOfActions=31,}
\PYG{g+go}{          numberOfCriteria=13,}
\PYG{g+go}{          weightDistribution=\PYGZsq{}equiobjectives\PYGZsq{},}
\PYG{g+go}{          seed=120)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showObjectives}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} show objectives \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZdq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Eco: Economical aspect}
\PYG{g+go}{   g04 criterion of objective Eco 20}
\PYG{g+go}{   g05 criterion of objective Eco 20}
\PYG{g+go}{   g08 criterion of objective Eco 20}
\PYG{g+go}{   g11 criterion of objective Eco 20}
\PYG{g+go}{  Total weight: 80.00 (4 criteria)}
\PYG{g+go}{Soc: Societal aspect}
\PYG{g+go}{   g06 criterion of objective Soc 16}
\PYG{g+go}{   g07 criterion of objective Soc 16}
\PYG{g+go}{   g09 criterion of objective Soc 16}
\PYG{g+go}{   g10 criterion of objective Soc 16}
\PYG{g+go}{   g13 criterion of objective Soc 16}
\PYG{g+go}{  Total weight: 80.00 (5 criteria)}
\PYG{g+go}{Env: Environmental aspect}
\PYG{g+go}{   g01 criterion of objective Env 20}
\PYG{g+go}{   g02 criterion of objective Env 20}
\PYG{g+go}{   g03 criterion of objective Env 20}
\PYG{g+go}{   g12 criterion of objective Env 20}
\PYG{g+go}{  Total weight: 80.00 (4 criteria)}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

In this example we notice that 5 equisignificant criteria (g06, g07, g09, g10, g13) evaluate for instance the performance of the decision actions from the societal point of view. 4 equisignificant criteria do the same from the economical, respectively the environmental point of view. The `equiobjectives' directive results hence in a balanced total weight (80.00) for each decision objective.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showActions}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  a01}
\PYG{g+go}{  name:       random decision action Eco+ Soc\PYGZhy{} Env+}
\PYG{g+go}{  profile:    \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}Eco\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}good\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}Soc\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}weak\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}Env\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}good\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  a02}
\PYG{g+gp}{...}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  a26}
\PYG{g+go}{  name:       random decision action Eco+ Soc+ Env\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{  profile:    \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}Eco\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}good\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}Soc\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}good\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}Env\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}weak\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{...}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  a30}
\PYG{g+go}{  name:       random decision action Eco\PYGZhy{} Soc\PYGZhy{} Env\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{  profile:    \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}Eco\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}weak\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}Soc\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}weak\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}Env\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}weak\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{...}
\end{Verbatim}

Variable triangular modes (0.3, 0.5 or 0.7 of the span of the measure scale) for each objective result in different performance status for each decision action with respect to the three objectives. For instance, action \emph{a01} will probably show \emph{good} performances wrt the \emph{economical}  and environmental aspects, and \emph{weak} performances wrt the \emph{societal} aspect.

For testing purposes a special constructor is provided for extracting partial performance tableaux from a given tableau instance. In this example we may construct the partial performance tableaux corresponding to each objective:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{perfTabs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{PartialPerformanceTableau}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{teco} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PartialPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{criteriaSubset}\PYG{o}{=}\PYGZbs{}
\PYG{g+go}{                          t.objectives[\PYGZsq{}Eco\PYGZsq{}][\PYGZsq{}criteria\PYGZsq{}])}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{tsoc} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PartialPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{criteriaSubset}\PYG{o}{=}\PYGZbs{}
\PYG{g+go}{                          t.objectives[\PYGZsq{}Soc\PYGZsq{}][\PYGZsq{}criteria\PYGZsq{}])}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{tenv} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PartialPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{criteriaSubset}\PYG{o}{=}\PYGZbs{}
\PYG{g+go}{                          t.objectives[\PYGZsq{}Env\PYGZsq{}][\PYGZsq{}criteria\PYGZsq{}])}
\end{Verbatim}

One may now compute partial bipolar outranking digraphs for each objective as follows:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{outrankingDigraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{geco} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{teco}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{gsoc} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{tsoc}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{genv} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{tenv}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{Verbatim}
\begin{description}
\item[{These three partial digraphs model the preferences represented in each of the partial performance tableaux. One may aggregate these three preferential with an epistemic fusion operator:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{digraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{FusionLDigraph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{gfus} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{FusionLDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{n}{geco}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{gsoc}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{genv}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{gfus}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{strongComponents}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZob{}frozenset(\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}a30\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ frozenset(\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}a10\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a03\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a19\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a08\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a07\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a04\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a21\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a20\PYGZsq{},}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}a13\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a23\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a16\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a12\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a24\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a02\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a31\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a29\PYGZsq{},}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}a05\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a09\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a28\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a25\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a17\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a14\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a15\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a06\PYGZsq{},}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}a01\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a27\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a11\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a18\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a22\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ frozenset(\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}a26\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{})\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{digraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{StrongComponentsCollapsedDigraph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{scc} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{StrongComponentsCollapsedDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{gfus}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{scc}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showActions}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} show digraphs actions \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  frozenset(\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}a30\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{})}
\PYG{g+go}{  short name: Scc\PYGZus{}1}
\PYG{g+go}{  name:       \PYGZus{}a30\PYGZus{}}
\PYG{g+go}{  comment:    collapsed strong component}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  frozenset(\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}a10\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a03\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a19\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a08\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a07\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a04\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a21\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a20\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a13\PYGZsq{},}
\PYG{g+go}{                 \PYGZsq{}a23\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a16\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a12\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a24\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a02\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a31\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a29\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a05\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a09\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a28\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a25\PYGZsq{},}
\PYG{g+go}{                 \PYGZsq{}a17\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a14\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a15\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a06\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a01\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a27\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a11\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a18\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a22\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{})}
\PYG{g+go}{  short name: Scc\PYGZus{}2}
\PYG{g+go}{  name:       \PYGZus{}a10\PYGZus{}a03\PYGZus{}a19\PYGZus{}a08\PYGZus{}a07\PYGZus{}a04\PYGZus{}a21\PYGZus{}a20\PYGZus{}a13\PYGZus{}a23\PYGZus{}a16\PYGZus{}a12\PYGZus{}a24\PYGZus{}a02\PYGZus{}a31\PYGZus{}\PYGZbs{}}
\PYG{g+go}{               a29\PYGZus{}a05\PYGZus{}a09\PYGZus{}a28\PYGZus{}a25\PYGZus{}a17\PYGZus{}a14\PYGZus{}a15\PYGZus{}a06\PYGZus{}a01\PYGZus{}a27\PYGZus{}a11\PYGZus{}a18\PYGZus{}a22\PYGZus{}}
\PYG{g+go}{  comment:    collapsed strong component}
\PYG{g+go}{key:  frozenset(\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}a26\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{})}

\end{Verbatim}

\item[{A graphviz drawing illustrates the apparent preferential links between the strong components:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{scc}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{scFusionObjectives}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} exporting a dot file for GraphViz tools \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Exporting to scFusionObjectives.dot}
\PYG{g+go}{dot \PYGZhy{}Grankdir=BT \PYGZhy{}Tpng scFusionObjectives.dot \PYGZhy{}o scFusionObjectives.png}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

{\hfill\includegraphics{sccFusionObjectives.png}\hfill}

Decision action \emph{a26} (Eco+ Soc+ Env-) appears dominating the other decision alternatives, whereas decision action \emph{a30} (Eco- Soc- Env-) appears to be dominated by all the others.

\subsubsection{What site to choose ?}
\label{tutorial:what-site-to-choose}
A SME, specialized in printing and copy services, has to move into new offices, and its CEO has gathered seven \textbf{potential office sites}:

\begin{description}
\item[{Three \textbf{decision objectives} are guiding the CEO's choice:}] \leavevmode\begin{enumerate}
\item {} 
\emph{minimize} the yearly costs induced by the moving,

\item {} 
\emph{maximize} the future turnover of the SME,

\item {} 
\emph{maximize} the new working conditions.

\end{enumerate}

\end{description}

Except the \emph{Costs} criterion, all other criteria admit for grading a qualitative satisfaction scale from 0\% (worst) to 100\% (best). We may thus notice that site \emph{A} is the most expensive, but also 100\% satisfying the \emph{Proximity} as well as the  \emph{Standing} criterion. Whereas the site \emph{C} is the cheapest one; providing however no satisfaction at all on both the \emph{Standing} and the \emph{Working Space} criteria.

All qualitative criteria, supporting their respective objective, are considered to be \emph{equi-significant} (weights = 1.0). As a consequence, the three objectives are considered \emph{equally important} (total weight = 3.0 each).

Concerning annual costs, we notice that the CEO is indifferent up to a performance difference of 1000\texteuro{}, and he actually prefers a site if there is at least a positive difference of 2500\texteuro{}. The grades observed on the six qualitative criteria (measured in percentages of satisfaction) are very subjective and rather imprecise. The CEO is hence indifferent up to a satisfaction difference of 10\%, and he claims a significant preference when the satisfaction difference is at least of 20\%.  Furthermore, a satisfaction difference of 80\% represents for him a \emph{considerably large} performance difference, triggering a \emph{veto} situation the case given (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id22}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2013]{\emph{{[}BIS-2013{]}}}}).

In view of this performance tableau, what is now the office site we may recommend to the CEO as \textbf{best choice} ?


\subsubsection{Performance tableau}
\label{tutorial:performance-tableau}
The XMCDA 2.0 encoded version of this performance tableau is available for downloading here officeChoice.xml.
\begin{quote}
\end{quote}
\begin{description}
\item[{We may inspect the performance tableau data with the computing resources provided by the {\hyperref[techDoc:perftabs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{perfTabs module}}}} module.}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{perfTabs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{o}{*}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{XMCDA2PerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{officeChoice}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{help}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{c}{\PYGZsh{} for discovering all the methods available}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}      \textbar{}    3.00   \textbar{} \PYGZhy{}35000.00 \PYGZhy{}17800.00 \PYGZhy{}6700.00 \PYGZhy{}14100.00 \PYGZhy{}34800.00 \PYGZhy{}18600.00 \PYGZhy{}12000.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}Cf\PYGZsq{}     \textbar{}    1.00   \textbar{}      0.00    100.00    10.00     30.00     60.00     80.00     50.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}P\PYGZsq{}      \textbar{}    1.00   \textbar{}     90.00     30.00   100.00     90.00     70.00      0.00     80.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}Pr\PYGZsq{}     \textbar{}    1.00   \textbar{}    100.00     20.00    80.00     70.00     40.00      0.00     60.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}St\PYGZsq{}     \textbar{}    1.00   \textbar{}    100.00     10.00     0.00     30.00     90.00     70.00     20.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}V\PYGZsq{}      \textbar{}    1.00   \textbar{}     60.00     80.00    70.00     50.00     60.00      0.00    100.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}W\PYGZsq{}      \textbar{}    1.00   \textbar{}     75.00     30.00     0.00     55.00    100.00      0.00     50.00}
\end{Verbatim}

\item[{We thus recover all the input data. To measure the actual preference discrimination we observe on each criterion, we may use the \code{showCriteria} method:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showCriteria}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}  criteria \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{C \PYGZsq{}Costs\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Scale = (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}0.00\PYGZsq{}), Decimal(\PYGZsq{}50000.00\PYGZsq{}))}
\PYG{g+go}{Weight = 0.333}
\PYG{g+go}{Threshold ind : 1000.00 + 0.00x ; percentile:  0.095}
\PYG{g+go}{Threshold pref : 2500.00 + 0.00x ; percentile:  0.143}
\PYG{g+go}{Cf \PYGZsq{}Comfort\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Scale = (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}0.00\PYGZsq{}), Decimal(\PYGZsq{}100.00\PYGZsq{}))}
\PYG{g+go}{Weight = 0.111}
\PYG{g+go}{Threshold ind : 10.00 + 0.00x ; percentile:  0.095}
\PYG{g+go}{Threshold pref : 20.00 + 0.00x ; percentile:  0.286}
\PYG{g+go}{Threshold veto : 80.00 + 0.00x ; percentile:  0.905}
\PYG{g+gp}{...}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

On the \emph{Costs} criterion, 9.5\% of the performance differences are considered insignificant and 14.3\% below the preference discrimination threshold (lines 6-7). On the qualitative \emph{Comfort} criterion, we observe again 9.5\% of insignificant performance differences (line 11). Due to the imprecision in the subjective grading, we notice here 28.6\% of performance differences below the preference discrimination threshold (line 12). Furthermore, 100.0 - 90.5 = 9.5\% of the performance differences are judged \emph{considerably large} (line 13); 80\% and more of satisfaction differences triggering in fact a veto situation. Same information is available for all the other criteria.
\begin{description}
\item[{A colorful comparison of all the performances is shown by the \textbf{heat map} statistics, illustrating the respective quantile class of each performance. As the set of potential alternatives is tiny, we choose here a classification into performance quintiles:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showHTMLPerformanceHeatmap}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{colorLevels}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

{\hfill\includegraphics{officeChoiceHeatmap.png}\hfill}

Site \emph{A} shows extreme and contradictory performances: highest \emph{Costs} and no \emph{Working Comfort} on one hand, and total satisfaction with respect to \emph{Standing}, \emph{Proximity} and \emph{Parking facilities} on the other hand. Similar, but opposite, situation is given for site \emph{C}: unsatisfactory \emph{Working Space}, no \emph{Standing} and no \emph{Working Comfort} on the one hand, and lowest \emph{Costs}, best \emph{Proximity} and \emph{Parking facilities} on the other hand. Contrary to these contradictory alternatives, we observe two appealing compromise decision alternatives: sites \emph{D} and \emph{G}. Finally, site \emph{F} is clearly the less satisfactory alternative of all.


\subsubsection{Outranking digraph}
\label{tutorial:id23}\begin{description}
\item[{To help now the CEO choosing the best site, we are going to compute pairwise outrankings (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id24}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2013]{\emph{{[}BIS-2013{]}}}}) on the set of potential sites. For two sites \emph{x} and \emph{y}, the situation ``\emph{x} outranks \emph{y}'', denoted (\emph{x} S \emph{y}), is given if there is:}] \leavevmode\begin{enumerate}
\item {} 
a \textbf{significant majority} of criteria concordantly supporting that site \emph{x} is \emph{at least as satisfactory as} site \emph{y}, and

\item {} 
\textbf{no considerable} counter-performance observed on any discordant criterion.

\end{enumerate}

\end{description}

The credibility of each pairwise outranking situation (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id25}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2013]{\emph{{[}BIS-2013{]}}}}), denoted r(\emph{x} S \emph{y}), is measured in a bipolar significance valuation {[}-100.00, 100.00{]}, where \textbf{positive} terms r(\emph{x} S \emph{y}) \textgreater{} 0.0 indicate a \textbf{validated}, and \textbf{negative} terms r(\emph{x} S \emph{y}) \textless{} 0.0 indicate a \textbf{non-validated} outrankings; whereas the \textbf{median} value r(\emph{x} S \emph{y}) = 0.0 represents an \textbf{indeterminate} situation.
\begin{description}
\item[{For computing such a bipolar valued outranking digraph from the given performance tableau \emph{t}, we use the \code{BipolarOutrankingDigraph} constructor from the {\hyperref[techDoc:outrankingdigraphs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{outrankingDigraphs module}}}} module. The \code{Digraph.showHTMLRelationTable} method shows here the resulting bipolar-valued adjacency matrix in a system browser window:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{outrankingDigraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showHTMLRelationTable}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

{\hfill\includegraphics{officeChoiceOutranking.png}\hfill}
\begin{description}
\item[{We may notice that Alternative \emph{D} is \textbf{positively outranking} all other potential office sites (a \emph{Condorcet winner}). Yet, alternatives \emph{A} (the most expensive) and \emph{C} (the cheapest) are \emph{not} outranked by any other site; they are in fact \textbf{weak} \emph{Condorcet winners}.}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{condorcetWinners}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{weakCondorcetWinners}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}]}
\end{Verbatim}

\item[{We may get even more insight in the apparent outranking situations when looking at the Condorcet digraph:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{officeChoice}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} exporting a dot file for GraphViz tools \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Exporting to officeChoice.dot}
\PYG{g+go}{dot \PYGZhy{}Grankdir=BT \PYGZhy{}Tpng officeChoice.dot \PYGZhy{}o officeChoice.png}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

{\hfill\includegraphics{officeChoice.png}\hfill}
\begin{description}
\item[{One may check that the outranking digraph \emph{g} does not admit in fact a cyclic strict preference situation:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeChordlessCircuits}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{[]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showChordlessCircuits}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{No circuits observed in this digraph.}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Chordless circuits \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{0 circuits.}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}


\subsubsection{Rubis best choice}
\label{tutorial:rubis-best-choice}\begin{description}
\item[{Following the Rubis outranking method (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id26}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2008]{\emph{{[}BIS-2008{]}}}}), potential best choice recommendations are determined by the outranking pre-kernels (weakly independent and strictly outranking choices) of the chordless odd circuits augmented outranking digraph. As we observe no circuits here, we may directly compute the pre-kernels of \emph{g}:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showPreKernels}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Computing preKernels \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Dominant preKernels :}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{   independence :  100.0}
\PYG{g+go}{   dominance    :  11.111}
\PYG{g+go}{   absorbency   :  \PYGZhy{}100.0}
\PYG{g+go}{   covering     :  1.000}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}B\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}E\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{   independence :  0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{   dominance    :  11.111}
\PYG{g+go}{   absorbency   :  \PYGZhy{}100.0}
\PYG{g+go}{   covering     :  0.500}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}G\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{   independence :  0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{   dominance    :  55.556}
\PYG{g+go}{   absorbency   :  0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{   covering     :  0.700}
\PYG{g+go}{Absorbent preKernels :}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}F\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{   independence :  0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{   dominance    :  0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{   absorbency   :  100.0}
\PYG{g+go}{   covering     :  0.700}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} statistics \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{graph name:  rel\PYGZus{}officeChoice.xml}
\PYG{g+go}{number of solutions}
\PYG{g+go}{ dominant kernels :  3}
\PYG{g+go}{ absorbent kernels:  1}
\PYG{g+go}{cardinality frequency distributions}
\PYG{g+go}{cardinality     :  [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]}
\PYG{g+go}{dominant kernel :  [0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0]}
\PYG{g+go}{absorbent kernel:  [0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]}
\PYG{g+go}{Execution time  : 0.00018 sec.}
\PYG{g+go}{Results in sets: dompreKernels and abspreKernels.}
\end{Verbatim}

\item[{We notice three potential best choice recommendations: the Condorcet winner \emph{D} (line 4), the triplet \emph{B}, \emph{C} and \emph{E} (line 9), and finally the pair \emph{A} and \emph{G} (line 14). The Rubis best choice recommendation is given by the \textbf{most determined} pre-kernel; the one supported by the most significant criteria coalition. This result is shown with the following command:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRubisBestChoiceRecommendation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{***********************}
\PYG{g+go}{* \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Rubis best choice recommendation(s) (BCR) \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{  (in decreasing order of determinateness)}
\PYG{g+go}{Credibility domain:  \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}min\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}100.0, \PYGZsq{}med\PYGZsq{}: 0.0, \PYGZsq{}max\PYGZsq{}: 100.0\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{ === \PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} potential BCR}
\PYG{g+go}{* choice              : [\PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{  +\PYGZhy{}irredundancy      : 100.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  independence        : 100.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  dominance           : 11.11}
\PYG{g+go}{  absorbency          : \PYGZhy{}100.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  covering (\PYGZpc{})        : 100.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  determinateness (\PYGZpc{}) : 56,0}
\PYG{g+go}{  characteristic vector = \PYGZob{} \PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}: 11.11, \PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}11.11, \PYGZsq{}B\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}11.11,}
\PYG{g+go}{         \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}11.11, \PYGZsq{}E\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}11.11, \PYGZsq{}F\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}11.11, \PYGZsq{}G\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}11.11 \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{ === \PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} potential BCR}

\PYG{g+go}{  +\PYGZhy{}irredundancy      : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  independence        : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  dominance           : 11.11}
\PYG{g+go}{  absorbency          : \PYGZhy{}100.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  covering (\PYGZpc{})        : 50.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  determinateness (\PYGZpc{}) : 50.0}
\PYG{g+go}{  \PYGZhy{} characteristic vector = \PYGZob{} \PYGZsq{}B\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}E\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}F\PYGZsq{}: 0.00,}
\PYG{g+go}{          \PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}G\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}: 0.00 \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{ === \PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} potential BCR}
\PYG{g+go}{* choice              : [\PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}G\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{  +\PYGZhy{}irredundancy      : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  independence        : 0.00}

\PYG{g+go}{  determinateness (\PYGZpc{}) : 50.0}
\PYG{g+go}{  \PYGZhy{} characteristic vector = \PYGZob{} \PYGZsq{}B\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}E\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}F\PYGZsq{}: 0.00,}
\PYG{g+go}{           \PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}G\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}: 0.00 \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{ === \PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} potential worst choice}

\PYG{g+go}{             \PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}G\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}: 0.00 \PYGZcb{}}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

We notice in line 7 above that the most significantly supported best choice recommendation is indeed the Condorcet winner \emph{D} with a majority of 56\% of the criteria significance (see line 13). Both other recommendation candidates, as well as the worst choice candidate are not positively validated as best choices. They may or may not be considered so. Alternative \emph{A}, with extreme contradictory performances, appears both, in a best and a worst choice recommendation (see lines 27 and 37) and seams hence not actually comparable to its competitors.
\begin{description}
\item[{The same Rubis best choice recommendation, encoded in XMCDA 2.0, is as well provided by the Rubis XMCDA 2.0 Web services available at the Leopold-Loewenheim Apache Server of the University of Luxembourg:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{outrankingDigraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{RubisRestServer}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{solver} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RubisRestServer}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{solver}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{ping}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*************************************************}
\PYG{g+go}{* This is the Leopold\PYGZhy{}Loewenheim Apache Server  *}
\PYG{g+go}{* of the University of Luxembourg.              *}
\PYG{g+go}{* Welcome to the Rubis XMCDA 2.0 Web service    *}
\PYG{g+go}{* R. Bisdorff (c) 2009\PYGZhy{}2013                     *}
\PYG{g+go}{* November 2013, version REST/D4 1.1            *}
\PYG{g+go}{*************************************************}
\end{Verbatim}

\item[{We may submit the given performance tableau:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{solver}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{submitProblem}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{The problem submission was successful !}
\PYG{g+go}{Server ticket: 1BYyGVwV866hSNZo}
\end{Verbatim}

\item[{With the given ticket, saved in a text file in the working directory, we may request from the Rubis solver the corresponding best choice recommendation:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{solver}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showSolution}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

and, in a system browser window, browse the solution file.
\begin{quote}
\end{quote}

Here, we find confirmed again that alternative \emph{D}, indeed, appears to be the most significant best choice candidate.

Yet, what about alternative \emph{G}, the other good compromise best choice we have noticed from the performance heat map shown above?


\subsubsection{Strictly best choice}
\label{tutorial:strictly-best-choice}\begin{description}
\item[{When comparing the performances of alternatives \emph{D} and \emph{G} on a pairwise perspective, we notice that, with the given preference discrimination thresholds, alternative \emph{G} is actually \textbf{certainly} \emph{at least as good as} alternative \emph{D} ( r(\emph{G} outranks \emph{D}) = 100.0).}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showPairwiseComparison}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{G}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{D}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}  pairwise comparison \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Comparing actions : (G, D)}
\PYG{g+go}{crit. wght.  g(x)      g(y)    diff.  \textbar{}   ind     pref    concord   \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{C   3.00 \PYGZhy{}12000.00 \PYGZhy{}14100.00 +2100.00 \textbar{} 1000.00 2500.00   +3.00     \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Cf  1.00     50.00     30.00   +20.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   +1.00     \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{P   1.00     80.00     90.00   \PYGZhy{}10.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   +1.00     \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Pr  1.00     60.00     70.00   \PYGZhy{}10.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   +1.00     \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{St  1.00     20.00     30.00   \PYGZhy{}10.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   +1.00     \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{V   1.00    100.00     50.00   +50.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   +1.00     \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{W   1.00     50.00     55.00    \PYGZhy{}5.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   +1.00     \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Valuation in range: \PYGZhy{}9.00 to +9.00; global concordance: +9.00}
\end{Verbatim}

\item[{However, we must as well notice that the cheapest alternative \emph{C} is in fact \textbf{strictly outranking} alternative \emph{G}:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showPairwiseComparison}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{C}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{G}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}  pairwise comparison \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Comparing actions : (C, G)/(G, C)}
\PYG{g+go}{crit. wght.   g(x)     g(y)      diff.  \textbar{}   ind.   pref.    (C,G)/(G,C) \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{C     3.00 \PYGZhy{}6700.00 \PYGZhy{}12000.00  +5300.00 \textbar{} 1000.00 2500.00   +3.00/\PYGZhy{}3.00 \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Cf    1.00    10.00     50.00    \PYGZhy{}40.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   \PYGZhy{}1.00/+1.00 \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{P     1.00   100.00     80.00    +20.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   +1.00/\PYGZhy{}1.00 \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Pr    1.00    80.00     60.00    +20.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   +1.00/\PYGZhy{}1.00 \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{St    1.00     0.00     20.00    \PYGZhy{}20.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   \PYGZhy{}1.00/+1.00 \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{V     1.00    70.00    100.00    \PYGZhy{}30.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   \PYGZhy{}1.00/+1.00 \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{W     1.00     0.00     50.00    \PYGZhy{}50.00 \textbar{}   10.00   20.00   \PYGZhy{}1.00/+1.00 \textbar{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}

\item[{To model these \emph{strict outranking} situations, we may compute the \textbf{codual}, the converse (\textasciitilde{}) of the dual (-), of the outranking digraph instance \emph{g} (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id27}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2013]{\emph{{[}BIS-2013{]}}}}). On this digraph, the Rubis best choice recommendation may be computed as follows:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{gcd} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{o}{\PYGZti{}}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{)}          \PYG{c}{\PYGZsh{}\PYGZsh{} g = BipolarOutrankingDigraph(t)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{gcd}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRubisBestChoiceRecommendation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{* \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Rubis best choice recommendation(s) \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{(in decreasing order of determinateness)}
\PYG{g+go}{Credibility domain:  \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}min\PYGZsq{}:\PYGZhy{}100.0, \PYGZsq{}max\PYGZsq{}: 100.0\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}med\PYGZsq{}:0.0\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{=== \PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} potential BCR}
\PYG{g+go}{* choice                : [\PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{  +\PYGZhy{}irredundancy        : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  independence          : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  dominance             : 11.11}
\PYG{g+go}{  absorbency            : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  covering (\PYGZpc{})          : 41.67}
\PYG{g+go}{  determinateness (\PYGZpc{})   : 0.53}
\PYG{g+go}{  characteristic vector :}
\PYG{g+go}{     \PYGZob{} \PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}: 11.11, \PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}G\PYGZsq{}: 0.00,}
\PYG{g+go}{       \PYGZsq{}B\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}11.11, \PYGZsq{}E\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}11.11, \PYGZsq{}F\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}11.11 \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{=== \PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} potential worst choice}
\PYG{g+go}{* choice                : [\PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}F\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{  +\PYGZhy{}irredundancy        : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  independence          : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  dominance             : \PYGZhy{}55.56}
\PYG{g+go}{  absorbency            : 100.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  covering (\PYGZpc{})          : 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{  determinateness (\PYGZpc{})   : 0.50}
\PYG{g+go}{  characteristic vector :}
\PYG{g+go}{     \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}B\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}: 0.00,}
\PYG{g+go}{      \PYGZsq{}E\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}F\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZsq{}G\PYGZsq{}: 0.00, \PYGZcb{}}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

It is interesting to notice that the \textbf{strict best choice recommendation} consists in the set of weak Condorcet winners: `A', `C' and `D' (see line 7). In the corresponding characteristic vector (see line 15-16), representing the bipolar credibility degree with which each alternative may indeed be considered a best choice (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id28}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2006]{\emph{{[}BIS-2006{]}}}}), we find confirmed that alternative \emph{D} is the only positively validated one, whereas both extreme alternatives - \emph{A} (the most expensive) and \emph{C} (the cheapest) - stay in an indeterminate situation. They may be potential best choice candidates besides \emph{D}. Notice furthermore that compromise alternative \emph{G}, while not actually included in the strict best choice recommendation, shows as well an indeterminate situation with respect to being or not a potential best choice candidate.

We may also notice that both alternatives \emph{A} and \emph{F} are reported as certainly outranked, hence a \textbf{worst choice recommendation}. This confirms the global incomparability status of alternative \emph{A}.


\subsubsection{Weakly ordering}
\label{tutorial:weakly-ordering}\begin{description}
\item[{To get a more complete insight in the overall strict outranking situations, we may use the {\hyperref[techDoc:weakOrders.RankingByChoosingDigraph]{\emph{\code{weakOrders.RankingByChoosingDigraph}}}} constructor imported from the {\hyperref[techDoc:weakorders-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{weakOrders module}}}} module, for computing a \textbf{ranking-by-choosing} result from the strict outranking digraph instance \emph{gcd}:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{weakOrders} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{RankingByChoosingDigraph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{rbc} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RankingByChoosingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{gcd}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Threading ...  \PYGZsh{}\PYGZsh{} multiprocessing if 2 cores are available}
\PYG{g+go}{Exiting computing threads}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{rbc}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRankingByChoosing}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Ranking by Choosing and Rejecting}
\PYG{g+go}{1st ranked [\PYGZsq{}D\PYGZsq{}] (0.28)}
\PYG{g+go}{   2nd ranked [\PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}G\PYGZsq{}] (0.17)}
\PYG{g+go}{   2nd last ranked [\PYGZsq{}B\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}C\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}E\PYGZsq{}] (0.22)}
\PYG{g+go}{1st last ranked [\PYGZsq{}A\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}F\PYGZsq{}] (0.50)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{rbc}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{officeChoiceRanking}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} exporting a dot file for GraphViz tools \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Exporting to officeChoiceRanking.dot}
\PYG{g+go}{0 \PYGZob{} rank = same; A; C; D; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{1 \PYGZob{} rank = same; G; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{2 \PYGZob{} rank = same; E; B; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{3 \PYGZob{} rank = same; F; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{dot \PYGZhy{}Grankdir=TB \PYGZhy{}Tpng officeChoiceRanking.dot \PYGZhy{}o officeChoiceRanking.png}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

{\hfill\includegraphics{officeChoiceRanking.png}\hfill}

In this \textbf{ranking-by-choosing} method, where we operate the epistemic fusion of iterated (strict) best and worst choices, compromise alternative \emph{D} is indeed ranked before compromise alternative \emph{G}. If the computing node supports multiple processor cores, best and worst choosing iterations are run in parallel. The overall partial ordering result shows again the important fact that the most expensive site \emph{A}, and the cheapest site \emph{C}, both appear incomparable with most of the other alternatives, as is apparent from the Hasse diagram (see above) of the ranking-by-choosing relation.

The best choice recommendation appears hence depending on the very importance the CEO is attaching to each of the three objectives he is considering. In the setting here, where he considers all three objectives to be \textbf{equally important} (minimize costs = 3.0, maximize turnover = 3.0, and maximize working conditions = 3.0), site \emph{D} represents actually the best compromise. However, if \emph{Costs} do not play much role, it would be perhaps better to decide to move to the most advantageous site \emph{A}; or if, on the contrary, \emph{Costs} do matter a lot, moving to the cheapest alternative \emph{C} could definitely represent a more convincing recommendation.

It might be worth, as an \textbf{exercise}, to modify on the one hand this importance balance in the XMCDA data file by lowering the significance of the \emph{Costs} criterion; all criteria are considered \textbf{equi-significant} (weight = 1.0) for instance. It may as well be opportune, on the other hand, to \textbf{rank} the importance of the three objectives as follows:  \emph{minimize costs} (weight = 9.0) \textbf{\textgreater{}} \emph{maximize turnover} (weight = 3 x 2.0) \textbf{\textgreater{}} \emph{maximize working conditions} (weight = 3 x 1.0). What will become the best choice recommendation under both working hypotheses?

Back to {\hyperref[tutorial:tutorial-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{Tutorials of the Digraph3 resources}}}}
