\subsubsection{The ranking problem}
\label{tutorial:the-ranking-problem}
We need to rank without ties a set \emph{X} of items (usually decision alternatives) that are evaluated on multiple incommensurable performance criteria; yet, for which we may know their pairwise valued outranking situation characteristics, i.e. r(\emph{x} S \emph{y}) for all \emph{x}, \emph{y} in \emph{X} (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id30}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2013]{\emph{{[}BIS-2013{]}}}}).

Unfortunately, the Condorcet digraph, associated with such a given outranking digraph, presents only exceptionally a linear ordering. Usually, pairwise majority comparisons do not render even a complete or, at least, a transitive partial outranking relation.

Let us consider a didactic outranking digraph generated from a random Cost-Benefit performance tableau concerning 9 decision alternatives evaluated on 13 performance criteria:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{outrankingDigraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{o}{*}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RandomCBPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{numberOfActions}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{9}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }                               \PYG{n}{numberOfCriteria}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{13}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{seed}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{Normalized}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRelationTable}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ReflexiveTerms}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{False}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{* \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Relation Table \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{ S   \textbar{}  \PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}    \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}    \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}   \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\textbar{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}    \PYGZhy{}     +0.00  +0.24  +0.24  +0.00  +0.17  +0.26  +0.07  +0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +0.00   \PYGZhy{}     \PYGZhy{}0.50  +0.00  \PYGZhy{}0.13  +0.00  +0.00  \PYGZhy{}0.02  +0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +0.14  +0.50   \PYGZhy{}     +0.40  +0.36  +0.50  +0.71  +0.69  +1.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +0.05  +0.00  \PYGZhy{}0.40   \PYGZhy{}     +0.00  +0.21  +0.26  \PYGZhy{}0.10  +0.10}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +0.00  +0.36  \PYGZhy{}0.36  +0.00   \PYGZhy{}     +0.26  +0.00  +0.26  \PYGZhy{}1.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   \PYGZhy{}0.10  +0.00  \PYGZhy{}0.29  \PYGZhy{}0.07  +0.02   \PYGZhy{}     +0.24  +0.19  +0.04}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   \PYGZhy{}0.26  +0.00  \PYGZhy{}0.29  \PYGZhy{}0.02  +0.00  \PYGZhy{}0.10   \PYGZhy{}     +0.00  \PYGZhy{}1.00}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   \PYGZhy{}0.07  +0.33  \PYGZhy{}0.24  +0.10  +0.05  +0.29  +0.00   \PYGZhy{}     \PYGZhy{}0.02}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +0.00  +0.00  \PYGZhy{}1.00  \PYGZhy{}0.10  +1.00  +0.33  +1.00  +0.02   \PYGZhy{}}
\end{Verbatim}

Some ranking rules will work on the associated Condorcet digraph, i.e. the strict median cut polarised digraph:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{c} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PolarisedOutrankingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{level}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{0}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{KeepValues}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{False}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }                               \PYG{n}{StrictCut}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{c}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRelationTable}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ReflexiveTerms}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{False}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{IntegerValues}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{* \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Relation Table \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{ S   \textbar{}  \PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{} \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{} \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{} \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{} \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{} \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{} \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{} \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{} \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{},}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\textbar{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}    \PYGZhy{}    0   +1   +1    0   +1   +1   +1    0}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}    0    \PYGZhy{}   \PYGZhy{}1    0   \PYGZhy{}1    0    0   \PYGZhy{}1    0}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +1   +1    \PYGZhy{}   +1   +1   +1   +1   +1   +1}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   +1    0   \PYGZhy{}1    \PYGZhy{}    0   +1   +1   \PYGZhy{}1   +1}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}    0   +1   \PYGZhy{}1    0    \PYGZhy{}   +1    0   +1   \PYGZhy{}1}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   \PYGZhy{}1    0   \PYGZhy{}1   \PYGZhy{}1   +1    \PYGZhy{}   +1   +1   +1}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   \PYGZhy{}1    0   \PYGZhy{}1   \PYGZhy{}1    0   \PYGZhy{}1    \PYGZhy{}    0   \PYGZhy{}1}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}   \PYGZhy{}1   +1   \PYGZhy{}1   +1   +1   +1    0    \PYGZhy{}   \PYGZhy{}1}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{} \textbar{}    0    0   \PYGZhy{}1   \PYGZhy{}1   +1   +1   +1   +1    \PYGZhy{}}
\end{Verbatim}

To estimate how difficult this ranking problem may be, we can have a look at the corresponding \emph{strict} outranking digraph graphviz drawing:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{gcd} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{o}{\PYGZti{}}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{c}{\PYGZsh{} converse of the negation of g}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{gcd}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{rankingTutorial}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} exporting a dot file for GraphViz tools \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Exporting to rankingTutorial.dot}
\PYG{g+go}{dot \PYGZhy{}Grankdir=BT \PYGZhy{}Tpng rankingTutorial.dot \PYGZhy{}o rankingTutorial.png}
\end{Verbatim}

{\hfill\includegraphics{rankingTutorial.png}\hfill}

The shown strict outranking relation is apparently not transitive: for instance, alternative \emph{a9} outranks alternative \emph{a5} and alternative \emph{a5} outranks \emph{a2}, however \emph{a9} does not outrank \emph{a2}. We may compute the transitivity degree of the outranking digraph, ie the ratio of the number of outranking arcs over the number of arcs of the transitive closue of the digraph \emph{g}:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeTransitivityDegree}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Decimal(\PYGZsq{}0.508\PYGZsq{})}
\end{Verbatim}

The outranking relation is hence very far from being transitive; a serious problem when a linear ordering of the decision alternatives is looked for. Let us furthermore see if there are any cyclic outrankings:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n+nb}{len}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeChordlessCircuits}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{1}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showChordlessCircuits}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Chordless circuits \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 0.024}
\end{Verbatim}

There is one chordless circuit detected in the given outranking digraph \emph{g}, namely \emph{a4} outranks \emph{a9}, the latter outranks \emph{a8}, and \emph{a8} outranks again \emph{a4}. Any potential linear ordering of these three alternatives will, in fact, always contradict somehow the given outranking relation.

Several heuristic ranking rules have been proposed for constructing a linear ordering which is closest in some specific sense to a given outranking relation.

The Digraph3 resources provide some of the most common of these ranking rules, like Copeland's, Kemeny's, Slater's, Kohler and Tideman's ranking rules.


\subsubsection{The Copeland ranking}
\label{tutorial:the-copeland-ranking}
Copeland's rule, the most intuitive one as it works well for any outranking relation which models in fact a linear order, computes for each alternative a score  resulting from the difference between its crisp out-degree (number of validated (+1) crisp outranking situations) and its crisp in-degree (number of validated crisp (+1) outranked situations):

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{linearOrders} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{CopelandOrder}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cop} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{CopelandOrder}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{Debug}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Copeland score for a1 = +3  (5 \PYGZhy{} 3)}
\PYG{g+go}{Copeland score for a2 = \PYGZhy{}3  (0 \PYGZhy{} 3)}
\PYG{g+go}{Copeland score for a3 = +7  (8 \PYGZhy{} 1)}
\PYG{g+go}{Copeland score for a4 = +1  (4 \PYGZhy{} 3)}
\PYG{g+go}{Copeland score for a5 = \PYGZhy{}1  (3 \PYGZhy{} 4)}
\PYG{g+go}{Copeland score for a6 = \PYGZhy{}2  (4 \PYGZhy{} 6)}
\PYG{g+go}{Copeland score for a7 = \PYGZhy{}5  (0 \PYGZhy{} 5)}
\PYG{g+go}{Copeland score for a8 = \PYGZhy{}1  (4 \PYGZhy{} 5)}
\PYG{g+go}{Copeland score for a9 = +1  (4 \PYGZhy{} 3)}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cop}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRanking}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}]}
\end{Verbatim}

Alternative \emph{a3} has the best score (+7), followed by alternative \emph{a1} (+3). Alternatives \emph{a4} and \emph{a9} have the same score (+1); following the lexicographic rule, \emph{a4} is hence ranked before \emph{a9}. Same situation is observed for \emph{a5} and \emph{a8} with a score of -1.

Notice by the way that Copeland scores, as computed in the associated Condorcet relation table or similarly in the codual digraph drawing above, are in fact invariant under a codual - converse of the negation \textasciitilde{}(-g) - transform of the outranking digraph.

Copeland's rule actually renders a linear order which is indeed highly correlated, in the ordinal Kendall sense (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id31}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2012]{\emph{{[}Bis-2012{]}}}}), with the given pairwise outranking relation:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{corr} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeOrdinalCorrelation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{cop}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s}{Fitness of Copeland}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{s ranking: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZpc{}.3f}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}} \PYG{o}{\PYGZpc{}} \PYG{n}{corr}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{correlation}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Fitness of Copeland\PYGZsq{}s ranking: 0.906}
\end{Verbatim}


\subsubsection{The Net-Flows ranking}
\label{tutorial:the-net-flows-ranking}
The valued version of the Copeland rule, called \textbf{Net-Flows} rule, is working directly on the given valued outranking digraph \emph{g}. For each alternative \emph{x} we compute a net flow score that is the sum of the differences between the \textbf{outranking} characteristics \(r(x\,S\,y)\) and the \textbf{outranked} characteristics r(\emph{y} S \emph{x}) for all pairs of alternatives where \emph{y} is different from \emph{x}:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{linearOrders} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{NetFlowsOrder}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{nf} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{NetFlowsOrder}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{nf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{netFlows}
\PYG{g+go}{[(Decimal(\PYGZsq{}7.143\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}2.155\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}1.214\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}\PYGZhy{}0.429\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}\PYGZhy{}0.690\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}\PYGZhy{}1.631\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}\PYGZhy{}1.774\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}\PYGZhy{}1.845\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{ (Decimal(\PYGZsq{}\PYGZhy{}4.143\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{})]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{nf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRanking}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{corr} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeOrdinalCorrelation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{nf}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s}{Fitness of net flows ranking: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZpc{}.3f}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}} \PYG{o}{\PYGZpc{}} \PYG{n}{corr}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{correlation}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Fitness of net flows ranking: 0.828}
\end{Verbatim}

The \textbf{Net-Flows} ranking is here, in this didactic example, not as much correlated with the given outranking relation as its crisp cousin ranking.

To appreciate the effective quality of both the Copeland and the Net-Flows rankings, it is useful to consider Kemeny's and Slater's optimal ranking rules.


\subsubsection{Kemeny rankings}
\label{tutorial:kemeny-rankings}
A \textbf{Kemeny} ranking is a linear order which is closest, in the sense of the ordinal Kendall distance (see \phantomsection\label{tutorial:id32}{\hyperref[tutorial:bis-2012]{\emph{{[}Bis-2012{]}}}}), to the given valued outranking digraph \emph{g}:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{linearOrders} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{KemenyOrder}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{ke} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{KemenyOrder}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{orderLimit}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{9}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{c}{\PYGZsh{} default orderLimit is 7}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{ke}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRanking}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{corr} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeOrdinalCorrelation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ke}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s}{Fitness of Kemeny}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{s ranking: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZpc{}.3f}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}} \PYG{o}{\PYGZpc{}} \PYG{n}{corr}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{correlation}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Fitness of Kemeny\PYGZsq{}s ranking: 0.9175}
\end{Verbatim}

So, \textbf{0.9175} is the highest possible ordinal correlation (fitness) any potential ranking can achieve with the given pairwise outranking relation. A Kemeny ranking may not be unique, and the first one discovered in a brute permutation trying computation, is retained. In our example we hence obtain seven optimal Kemeny rankings with a same maximal Kemeny index of 15.095:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{ke}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{maximalRankings}
\PYG{g+go}{[[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}],}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}],}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}],}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}],}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}],}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}],}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}]]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{ke}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{maxKemenyIndex}
\PYG{g+go}{Decimal(\PYGZsq{}15.095\PYGZsq{})}
\end{Verbatim}

We may visualize the partial order defined by the epistemic disjunction of these seven Kemeny rankings (see weakOrders module) as follows:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{weakOrders} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{KemenyWeakOrder}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{wke} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{KemenyWeakOrder}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{orderLimit}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{9}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{wke}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{tutorialKemeny}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} exporting a dot file for GraphViz tools \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Exporting to tutorialKemeny.dot}
\PYG{g+go}{0 \PYGZob{} rank = same; a1; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{1 \PYGZob{} rank = same; a3; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{2 \PYGZob{} rank = same; a4; a9; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{3 \PYGZob{} rank = same; a5; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{4 \PYGZob{} rank = same; a8; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{5 \PYGZob{} rank = same; a2; a6; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{6 \PYGZob{} rank = same; a7; \PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{dot \PYGZhy{}Grankdir=TB \PYGZhy{}Tpng tutorialKemeny.dot \PYGZhy{}o tutorialKemeny.png}
\end{Verbatim}

{\hfill\includegraphics[width=100pt]{tutorialKemeny.png}\hfill}

It is interesting to notice that all seven Kemeny rankings place alternative \emph{a1} at rank 1 before alternative \emph{a3}. This is precisely the only inversion that separates the Copeland ranking (see above) from being optimal in the Kemeny sense.


\subsubsection{Slater rankings}
\label{tutorial:slater-rankings}
The \textbf{Slater} ranking rule is similar to Kemeny's, but it is working, instead,  on the associated crisp Condorcet digraph \emph{c}. It renders here the following results:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{sl} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{KemenyOrder}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{c}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{orderLimit}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{9}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n+nb}{len}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{sl}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{maximalRankings}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{174}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{sl}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRanking}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{corr} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeOrdinalCorrelation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{sl}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s}{Fitness of Slater}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{s ranking: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZpc{}.3f}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}} \PYG{o}{\PYGZpc{}} \PYG{n}{corr}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{correlation}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Fitness of Slater\PYGZsq{}s ranking: 0.844}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{slw} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{KemenyWeakOrder}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{c}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{orderLimit}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{9}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{slw}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{tutorialSlater}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{Verbatim}

We notice that the first crisp Slater ranking is a rather good fit (0.844), better apparently than the Net-Flows ranking. However, there are in fact 174 such potentially optimal Slater rankings. The corresponding epistemic disjunction gives the follwowing partial ordering:

{\hfill\includegraphics[width=150pt]{tutorialSlater.png}\hfill}

What precise ranking result should we hence adopt ?

Kemeny's as well as Slater's ranking rules are furthermore computationally difficult problems and effective ranking results are only computable for tiny outranking digraphs (\textless{} 15 objects).

More efficient ranking heuristics, like the Copeland and the Net-Flows rules, are therefore needed in practice.


\subsubsection{Kohler's ranking-by-choosing rule}
\label{tutorial:kohler-s-ranking-by-choosing-rule}
Kohler's \textbf{ranking-by-choosing} rule can be formulated like this.

At step \emph{r} (\emph{r} goes from 1 to \emph{n}) do the following:
\begin{quote}
\begin{enumerate}
\item {} 
Compute for each row of the valued outranking relation table (see above) the smallest value;

\item {} 
Select the row where this minimum is maximal. Ties are resolved in lexicographic order;

\item {} 
Put the selected decision alternative at rank \emph{r};

\item {} 
Delete the corresponding row and column from the relation table and restart until the table is empty.

\end{enumerate}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{linearOrders} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{KohlerOrder}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{ko} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{KohlerOrder}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{ko}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRanking}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{corr} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeOrdinalCorrelation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ko}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s}{Fitness of Kohler}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{s ranking: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZpc{}.3f}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}} \PYG{o}{\PYGZpc{}} \PYG{n}{corr}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{correlation}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Fitness of Kohler\PYGZsq{}s ranking: 0.868}
\end{Verbatim}
\end{quote}

Here, we find a better fitness (0.868) when compared with Slater's (0.844) or the Net-Flows result (0.828), but not as good as Copeland crisp rule's result (0.906).


\subsubsection{Tideman's Ranked-Pairs rule}
\label{tutorial:tideman-s-ranked-pairs-rule}
A further ranking heuristic, the \textbf{Ranked-Pairs} rule, is based on a prudent incremental construction of linear orders that avoids on the fly any cycling outrankings. The ranking procedure may be formulated as follows:
\begin{enumerate}
\item {} 
Rank the ordered pairs \((x,y)\) of alternatives in decreasing order of the outranking characteristic values \(r(x\,S\,y)\);

\item {} 
Consider the pairs in that order (ties are resolved by a lexicographic rule):
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
if the next pair does not create a cycle with the pairs already blocked, block this pair;

\item {} 
if the next pair creates a cycle with the already blocked pairs, skip it.

\end{itemize}

\end{enumerate}

In our didactic outranking example, we get the following result:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\},numbers=left,firstnumber=1,stepnumber=1]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{linearOrders} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{RankedPairsOrder}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{rp} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RankedPairsOrder}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{Debug}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{next pair:  (\PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}) 1.00}
\PYG{g+go}{added: (a3,a9) characteristic: 1.00 (1.0)}
\PYG{g+go}{added: (a9,a3) characteristic: \PYGZhy{}1.00 (\PYGZhy{}1.0)}
\PYG{g+go}{   ...}
\PYG{g+go}{   ...}
\PYG{g+go}{next pair:  (\PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}) 0.09523809523809523809523809524}
\PYG{g+go}{Circuit detected !!}
\PYG{g+go}{next pair:  (\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}) 0.07142857142857142857142857143}
\PYG{g+go}{added: (a1,a8) characteristic: 0.07 (1.0)}
\PYG{g+go}{added: (a8,a1) characteristic: \PYGZhy{}0.07 (\PYGZhy{}1.0)}
\PYG{g+go}{   ...}
\PYG{g+go}{   ...}
\PYG{g+go}{next pair:  (\PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}) 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{Circuit detected !!}
\PYG{g+go}{next pair:  (\PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}) 0.00}
\PYG{g+go}{added: (a2,a6) characteristic: 0.00 (1.0)}
\PYG{g+go}{added: (a6,a2) characteristic: 0.00 (\PYGZhy{}1.0)}
\PYG{g+go}{   ...}
\PYG{g+go}{   ...}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{rp}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRanking}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a9\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a8\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}a7\PYGZsq{}]}
\end{Verbatim}

The Ranked-Pairs rule actually renders one of the seven optimal Kemeny rankings as we may verify below:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{corr} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeOrdinalCorrelation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rp}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s}{Fitness of Tideman}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{s ranking: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZpc{}.3f}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}} \PYG{o}{\PYGZpc{}} \PYG{n}{corr}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{correlation}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Fitness of Tideman\PYGZsq{}s ranking: 0.918}
\end{Verbatim}

Unfortunately, the Ranked-Pairs ranking rule is again not efficiently scalable to outranking digraphs of larger orders (\textgreater{} 100). For such outranking digraphs, with several hundred of alternatives, only the Copeland and the Net-Flows ranking rules, with a polynomial complexity of \(O(n^2)\) where \emph{n} is the order of the outranking digraph, remain in fact computationally efficient.

\subsection{Introduction}
\label{techDoc:technical-label}\label{techDoc:introduction}
\textbf{Dowloading the Digraph3 ressources}

Three download options are given:
\begin{enumerate}
\item {} 
Either (easiest under Linux or Mac OS-X), by using a git client:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
..\PYGZdl{} git clone https://github.com/rbisdorff/Digraph3
\end{Verbatim}

\item {} 
or a subversion client:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
..\PYGZdl{} svn co http://leopold\PYGZhy{}loewenheim.uni.lu/svn/repos/Digraph3
\end{Verbatim}

\item {} 
Or, with a browser access, download and extract the latest distribution tar.gz archive from this page:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
http://leopold\PYGZhy{}loewenheim.uni.lu/Digraph3/dist/
\end{Verbatim}

\end{enumerate}

Developping the Rubis decision support methodology is an ongoing research project of Raymond Bisdorff \textless{}\href{http://leopold-loewenheim.uni.lu/bisdorff/}{http://leopold-loewenheim.uni.lu/bisdorff/}\textgreater{}, University of Luxembourg.

To be fully functional, the Digraph3 resources mainly need the \href{http://graphviz.org}{graphviz} tools and the \href{http://www.r-project.org}{R statistics resources} to be installed. When exploring digraph isomorphisms, the \href{http://www.cs.sunysb.edu/~algorith/implement/nauty/implement.shtml}{nauty} isomorphism testing program is required. two specific criteria and actions clustering methods of the Digraph class furthermore require the \href{http://leopold-loewenheim.uni.lu/svn/repos/Calmat/}{calmat} matrix computing resource to be installed.

\textbf{Organisation of the Digraph3 python3 source code}

The Digraph3 source code is split into several interdependent modules of which the \code{digraphs} module is the master module.
\begin{itemize}
\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:digraphs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{digraphs module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Main part of the Digraph3 source code with the root Digraph class;

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:graphs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{graphs module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Specialization for undirected graphs with the root Graph class and a brigde to the \code{digraphs} module resources;

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:outrankingdigraphs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{outrankingDigraphs module}}}}}] \leavevmode
New Python3 specific root OutrankingDigraph class and specializations;

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:perftabs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{perfTabs module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Everything needed for handling Multiple Criteria Decision Aid performance tableaux with root PerformaceTableau class;

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:votingdigraphs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{votingDigraphs module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Additional classes and methods for computing election results with main LinearVotingProfile class;

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:sortingdigraphs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{sortingDigraphs module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Additional tools for solving sorting problems with the root SortingDigraph class;

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:linearorders-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{linearOrders module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Additional tools for solving linearly ranking problems with the root LinearOrder class;

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:weakorders-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{weakOrders module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Additional tools for solving ranking by choosing problems with root WeakOrder class.

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:randomnumbers-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{randomNumbers module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Additional random number generators, not available in the standard python library.

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:randomdigraphs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{randomDigraphs module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Various implemented random digraph models.

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:randomperftabs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{randomPerfTabs module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Various implemented random performance tableau models.

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{{\hyperref[techDoc:bigoutrankingdigraphs-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{bigOutrankingDigraphs module}}}}}] \leavevmode
Compressed implementation design for large outranking digraphs (order \textgreater{} 1000).

\end{description}

\end{itemize}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.CirculantDigraph}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{digraphs.}\bfcode{CirculantDigraph}}{\emph{order=7, valuationdomain=\{`max': Decimal(`1.0'), `min': Decimal(`-1.0')\}, circulants={[}-1, 1{]}}}{}
Bases: {\hyperref[techDoc:digraphs.Digraph]{\emph{\code{digraphs.Digraph}}}}
\begin{description}
\item[{Parameters:}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] order \textgreater{} 0;

and/or negative circular shifts of value 1 to n.

Specialization of the general Digraph class for generating
temporary circulant digraphs.
\begin{description}
\item[{Default instantiation C\_7:}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] order = 7,
\item[] valuationdomain = \{`min':-1.0,'max':1.0\},
\item[] circulants = {[}-1,1{]}.
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

Example session:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{digraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{CirculantDigraph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{c8} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{CirculantDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{order}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{8}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{circulants}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{c8}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{c8}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} exporting a dot file dor GraphViz tools \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Exporting to c8.dot}
\PYG{g+go}{circo \PYGZhy{}Tpng c8.dot \PYGZhy{}o c8.png}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZsh{} see below the graphviz drawing}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{c8}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showChordlessCircuits}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{No circuits yet computed. Run computeChordlessCircuits()!}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{c8}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{computeChordlessCircuits}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{...}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{c8}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showChordlessCircuits}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Chordless circuits \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}7\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}8\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}6\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}8\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}6\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}6\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}8\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}7\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}7\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}7\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}6\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}7\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}8\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}7\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}8\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{[\PYGZsq{}3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}8\PYGZsq{}] , credibility : 1.0}
\PYG{g+go}{12 circuits.}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\end{Verbatim}

{\hfill\includegraphics{c8.png}\hfill}

\index{CocaDigraph (class in digraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.CocaDigraph}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{digraphs.}\bfcode{CocaDigraph}}{\emph{digraph=None}, \emph{Cpp=False}, \emph{Piping=False}, \emph{Comments=False}}{}
Bases: {\hyperref[techDoc:digraphs.Digraph]{\emph{\code{digraphs.Digraph}}}}
\begin{description}
\item[{Parameters:}] \leavevmode
Stored or memory resident digraph instance.

\end{description}

Specialization of general Digraph class for instantiation
of chordless odd circuits augmented digraphs.

A chordless odd circuit is added if the cumulated credibility of the circuit supporting arcs is larger or
equal to the cumulated credibility of the converse arcs. Otherwise, the circuit is broken at the weakest asymmetric link,
i.e. a link (\emph{x},*y*) with minimal difference between r(\emph{x} S \emph{y}) - r(\emph{y} S \emph{x}).
\index{addCircuits() (digraphs.CocaDigraph method)}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{iterateCocElimination() (digraphs.CoceDigraph method)}

Eliminates all chordless odd circuits with rising valuation cut levels.
Renders a tuple (level,polarisedDigraph) where level is the

\index{Digraph (class in digraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{digraphs.}\bfcode{Digraph}}{\emph{file=None}, \emph{order=7}}{}
Bases: \href{http://docs.python.org/library/functions.html\#object}{\code{object}}

General class of digraphs, R.B. March 2006:
\begin{description}
\item[{Python data file format:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
actionset = {[}`1',`2',`3',`4',`5'{]}

\item {} 
valuationdomain = \{ `min':0, `med':1, `max': 2\}

\item {} 
relation = \{ `1': \{ `1':0, `2': 2, ...\}, ...\}

\end{itemize}

\item[{Example python3 (3.3+ recommended) session::}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{digraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{Digraph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{Digraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{tempdigraph}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showShort}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} show short \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Digraph          : tempdigraph}
\PYG{g+go}{Actions          : [\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}3\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{Valuation domain : \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}med\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZdq{}0.5\PYGZdq{}), \PYGZsq{}max\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZdq{}1.0\PYGZdq{}), \PYGZsq{}min\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZdq{}0\PYGZdq{})\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Connected Components \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{1: [\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}3\PYGZsq{}]}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

\index{computeChordlessCircuits() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computeChordlessCircuits}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeChordlessCircuits}}{\emph{Odd=False}, \emph{Comments=False}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Renders the set of all chordless odd circuits detected in a digraph.
Result (possible empty list) stored in \textless{}self.circuitsList\textgreater{}
holding a possibly empty list tuples with at position 0 the
list of adjacent actions of the circuit and at position 1
the set of actions in the stored circuit.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeChordlessCircuitsMP() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computeChordlessCircuitsMP}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeChordlessCircuitsMP}}{\emph{Odd=False}, \emph{Threading=False}, \emph{nbrOfCPUs=None}, \emph{Comments=False}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Multiprocessing version of computeChordlessCircuits().

Renders the set of all chordless odd circuits detected in a digraph.
Result (possible empty list) stored in \textless{}self.circuitsList\textgreater{}
holding a possibly empty list tuples with at position 0 the
list of adjacent actions of the circuit and at position 1
the set of actions in the stored circuit.
Inspired by Dias, Castonguay, Longo, Jradi, Algorithmica (2015).

Returns a possibly empty list of tuples (circuit,frozenset(circuit)).

If Odd == True, only circuits of odd length are retained in the result.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeOrdinalCorrelation() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computeOrdinalCorrelation}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeOrdinalCorrelation}}{\emph{other}, \emph{MedianCut=False}, \emph{filterRelation=None}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Renders the bipolar correlation K of a
self.relation when compared
with a given compatible (same actions set)) digraph or
a {[}-1,1{]} valued compatible relation (same actions set).

If MedianCut=True, the correlation is computed on the median polarized relations.

If filterRelation != None, the correlation is computed on the partial domain corresponding to the determined part of the filter relation.

\begin{notice}{warning}{Warning:}
Notice that the `other' relation and/or the `filterRelation',
the case given, must both be normalized, ie {[}-1,1{]}-valued !
\end{notice}

K = sum\_\{x != y\} {[} min( max(-self.relation{[}x{]}{[}y{]}),other.relation{[}x{]}{[}y{]}), max(self.relation{[}x{]}{[}y{]},-other.relation{[}x{]}{[}y{]}) {]}

K /= sum\_\{x!=y\} {[} min(abs(self.relation{[}x{]}{[}y{]}),abs(other.relation{[}x{]}{[}y{]})) {]}

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}
Renders a tuple with at position 0 the actual bipolar correlation index
and in position 1 the minimal determination level D of self and
the other relation.

D = sum\_\{x != y\} min(abs(self.relation{[}x{]}{[}y{]}),abs(other.relation{[}x{]}{[}y{]})) / n(n-1)

where n is the number of actions considered.

The correlation index with a completely indeterminate relation
is by convention 0.0 at determination level 0.0 .
\end{notice}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeOrdinalCorrelationMP() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computeOrdinalCorrelationMP}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeOrdinalCorrelationMP}}{\emph{other}, \emph{MedianCut=False}, \emph{Threading=True}, \emph{nbrOfCPUs=None}, \emph{Comments=False}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Multi processing version of the digraphs.computeOrdinalCorrelation() method.

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}
The relation filtering and the MedinaCut option are not implemented in the MP version.
\end{notice}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computePairwiseClusterComparison() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computePairwiseClusterComparison}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computePairwiseClusterComparison}}{\emph{K1}, \emph{K2}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
compute the pairwise cluster comparison credibility vector
from bipolar-valued digraph g. with K1 and K2 disjoint
lists of action keys from g actions disctionary.
Returns the dictionary
\{`I': Decimal(),'P+':Decimal(),'P-`:Decimal(),'R' :Decimal()\}
where one and only one item is strictly positive.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computePrincipalOrder() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computePrincipalOrder}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computePrincipalOrder}}{\emph{plotFileName=None}, \emph{Colwise=False}, \emph{imageType=None}, \emph{TempDir=None}, \emph{Comments=False}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
renders a ordered list of self.actions using the decreasing scores from the
first rincipal eigenvector of the covariance of the valued outdegrees of self.

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}
The method, relying on writing and reading temporary files by default in a temporary directory is threading and multiprocessing safe !
(see Digraph.exportPrincipalImage method)
\end{notice}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeRankingByBestChoosing() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computeRankingByBestChoosing}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeRankingByBestChoosing}}{\emph{CoDual=False}, \emph{CppAgrum=False}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Computes a weak preordering of the self.actions by recursive
best choice elagations.

Stores in self.rankingByBestChoosing{[}'result'{]} a list of (P+,bestChoice) tuples
where P+ gives the best choice complement outranking
average valuation via the computePairwiseClusterComparison
method.

If self.rankingByBestChoosing{[}'CoDual'{]} is True, 
the ranking-by-choosing was computed on the codual of self.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeRankingByBestChoosingRelation() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computeRankingByBestChoosingRelation}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeRankingByBestChoosingRelation}}{\emph{rankingByBestChoosing=None}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Renders the bipolar-valued relation obtained from
the self.rankingByBestChoosing result.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeRankingByChoosing() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computeRankingByChoosing}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeRankingByChoosing}}{\emph{actionsSubset=None}, \emph{CppAgrum=False}, \emph{Debug=False}, \emph{CoDual=False}}{}
Computes a weak preordring of the self.actions by iterating
jointly best and worst choice elagations.

Stores in self.rankingByChoosing{[}'result'{]} a list of ((P+,bestChoice),(P-,worstChoice)) pairs
where P+ (resp. P-) gives the best (resp. worst) choice complement outranking
(resp. outranked) average valuation via the computePairwiseClusterComparison
method.

If self.rankingByChoosing{[}'CoDual'{]} is True, the ranking-by-choosing was computed on the codual of self.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeRankingByChoosingRelation() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computeRankingByChoosingRelation}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeRankingByChoosingRelation}}{\emph{rankingByChoosing=None}, \emph{actionsSubset=None}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Renders the bipolar-valued relation obtained from
the self.rankingByChoosing result.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeRankingByLastChoosing() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.computeRankingByLastChoosing}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeRankingByLastChoosing}}{\emph{CoDual=False}, \emph{CppAgrum=False}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Computes a weak preordring of the self.actions by iterating
worst choice elagations.

Stores in self.rankingByLastChoosing{[}'result'{]} a list of (P-,worstChoice) pairs
where P- gives the worst choice complement outranked
average valuation via the computePairwiseClusterComparison
method.

If self.rankingByChoosing{[}'CoDual'{]} is True, the ranking-by-last-chossing 
was computed on the codual of self.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{determinateness() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.determinateness}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{determinateness}}{\emph{vec}, \emph{inPercent=True}}{}
Renders the determinateness of a bipolar characteristic vector
{[}(r(x),x),(r(y),y), ...{]} of length n in valuationdomain {[}Min,Max{]}:

result = sum\_x abs(r(x))/(n*(Max-Min)

If inPercent, result shifted (+1) and reduced (/2) to {[}0,1{]}.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{exportD3() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.exportD3}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{exportD3}}{\emph{fileName='index'}, \emph{Comments=True}}{}
This function was designed and implemented by Gary Cornelius, 2014 for his bachelor thesis at the University of Luxembourg. 
The thesis document with more explanations can be found
\href{http://leopold-loewenheim.uni.lu/Digraph3/literature/}{here} .
\begin{description}
\item[{\emph{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
fileName, name of the generated html file, default = None (graph name as defined in python);

\item {} 
Comments, True = default;

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

The idea of the project was to find a way that allows you to easily get details about certain nodes or edges of a directed graph in a dynamic format. 
Therefore this function allows you to export a html file together with all the needed libraries, including the 
D3 Library which we use for graph generation and the physics between nodes, which attracts or pushes nodes away from each other.
\begin{description}
\item[{Features of our graph include i.e.}] \leavevmode{[}{]}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
A way to only inspect a node and it's neighbours

\item {} 
Dynamic draging and freezing of the graph

\item {} 
Export of a newly created general graph

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

You can find the list of fututres in the Section below which is arranged according to the graph type.
\begin{description}
\item[{\emph{If the graph is an outrankingdigraphs}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
Nodes can be dragged and only the name and comment can be edited.

\item {} 
Edges can be inspected but not edited for this purpose a special json array containing all possible pairwiseComparisions is generated.

\end{itemize}

\item[{\emph{If the graph is a general graph}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
Nodes can be dragged, added, removed and edited.

\item {} 
Edges can be added, removed, inverted and edited. But edges cannot be inspected.

\item {} 
The pairwiseComparisions key leads to an empty array \{\}.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

In both cases, undefined edges can be hidden and reappear after a simple reload.(right click - reload)
\begin{description}
\item[{\emph{The generated files}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
d3.v3.js, contains the D3 Data-driven Documents source code, containing one small addition that we made in order to be able to easyly import links with a different formatself.

\item {} 
digraph3lib.js, contains our library. This file contains everything that we need from import of an XMCDA2 file, visualization of the graph to export of the changed graph.

\item {} 
d3export.json, usually named after the python graph name followed by a ticket number if the file is already present. It is the JSON file that is exported with the format ``\{``xmcda2'': ``some xml'',''pairwiseComparisions'':''\{``a01'': ``some html'',...\}''\}.

\end{itemize}

\item[{\emph{Example 1}:}] \leavevmode\begin{enumerate}
\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{python3 session:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{digraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{RandomValuationDigraph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{dg} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RandomValuationDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{order}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{Normalized}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{dg}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportD3}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{or}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} dg.showInteractiveGraph()}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{index.html:   }] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{Main Screen:}] \leavevmode
\includegraphics{randomvaluation_d3_main.png}

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{Inspect function:}] \leavevmode
\includegraphics{randomvaluation_d3_inspect.png}

\end{description}

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

\end{enumerate}

\end{description}

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}
If you want to use the automatic load in Chrome, try using the command: ``python -m SimpleHTTPServer''
and then access the index.html via ``\href{http://0.0.0.0:8000/index.html}{http://0.0.0.0:8000/index.html}''.
In order to load the CSS an active internet connection is needed!
\end{notice}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{showRelationMap() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.showRelationMap}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{showRelationMap}}{\emph{symbols=None}, \emph{rankingRule='netFlows'}}{}
Prints on the console, in text map format, the location of
certainly validated and certainly invalidated outranking situations.
\begin{description}
\item[{By default, symbols = \{`max':'┬','positive': `+', `median': ` `,}] \leavevmode
`negative': `-`, `min': `┴'\}

\end{description}

The default ordering of the output is following the net flows ranking rule
from best to worst actions. Further available ranking rules are Kohler's (rankingRule=''kohler'')
and Tideman's ranked pairs rule (rankingRule=''rankedPairs'')

Example:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{outrankingDigraphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{o}{*}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RandomCBPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{numberOfActions}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{25}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{seed}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{Normalized}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{gcd} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{o}{\PYGZti{}}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{)}  \PYG{c}{\PYGZsh{} strict outranking relation}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{gcd}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showRelationMap}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rankingRule}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s}{netFlows}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{ \PYGZhy{}   ++++++++  +++++┬+┬┬+}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}   + +++++ ++┬+┬+++┬++}
\PYG{g+go}{┴+  ┴ + ++  ++++┬+┬┬++┬++}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}   ++ \PYGZhy{} ++++\PYGZhy{}++++++┬++┬+}
\PYG{g+go}{   \PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{} ++\PYGZhy{} ┬\PYGZhy{} + \PYGZhy{}+┬+\PYGZhy{}┬┬}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}  \PYGZhy{}      \PYGZhy{}┬┬┬\PYGZhy{}+  \PYGZhy{}┬┬}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}    \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{}+++++++++++++}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}++ ++ +++\PYGZhy{}┬+\PYGZhy{}┬┬}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}  \PYGZhy{}  \PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} ++\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+++++ +}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} ++\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} +\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}++++┬ +}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+ \PYGZhy{}++\PYGZhy{}+++\PYGZhy{}+ +\PYGZhy{}++}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+  + +\PYGZhy{}++\PYGZhy{}+ \PYGZhy{}  +}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} ┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} ++\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}++++ \PYGZhy{} +}
\PYG{g+go}{  \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}  \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}  \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+ \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┬┬}
\PYG{g+go}{ \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+ \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} +\PYGZhy{}┬┬}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{} + \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+++┬ +}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}┴┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} ++ \PYGZhy{}++\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+++}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{} ++\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+ }
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}┴┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}++┬ }
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+┬ }
\PYG{g+go}{┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴┴ \PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴┴\PYGZhy{}┴ \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}++  ++\PYGZhy{}+}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}  }
\PYG{g+go}{┴┴┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{}┴+┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{}+\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}+ ┴+}
\PYG{g+go}{┴┴\PYGZhy{}┴┴┴\PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}┴┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴┴+ ┬ \PYGZhy{}}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴┴\PYGZhy{}┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}┴┴\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}  \PYGZhy{} \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} }
\PYG{g+go}{Ranking rule: netFlows}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }
\end{Verbatim}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{topologicalSort() (digraphs.Digraph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.topologicalSort}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{topologicalSort}}{\emph{Debug=False}}{}
If self is acyclic, adds topological sort number to each node of self
and renders ordered list of nodes. Otherwise renders None.
Source: M. Golumbic Algorithmic Graph heory and Perfect Graphs,
Annals Of Discrete Mathematics 57 2nd Ed. , Elsevier 2004, Algorithm 2.4 p.44.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{DualDigraph (class in digraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.DualDigraph}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{digraphs.}\bfcode{DualDigraph}}{\emph{other}}{}
Bases: {\hyperref[techDoc:digraphs.Digraph]{\emph{\code{digraphs.Digraph}}}}

Instantiates the dual Digraph object of a given other Digraph instance.
\begin{description}
\item[{The relation constructor returns the dual of self.relation with formula:}] \leavevmode
relationOut{[}a{]}{[}b{]} = Max - self.relation{[}a{]}{[}b{]} + Min
where Max (resp. Min) equals valuation maximum (resp. minimum).

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{PolarisedDigraph (class in digraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.PolarisedDigraph}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{digraphs.}\bfcode{PolarisedDigraph}}{\emph{digraph=None}, \emph{level=None}, \emph{KeepValues=True}, \emph{AlphaCut=False}, \emph{StrictCut=False}}{}
Bases: {\hyperref[techDoc:digraphs.Digraph]{\emph{\code{digraphs.Digraph}}}}

Renders the polarised valuation of a Digraph class instance:
\begin{description}
\item[{\emph{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
If level = None, a default 75\% cut level (0.5 in a normalized valuation domain) is used.

\item {} 
If KeepValues = False, the polarisation results in  a three valued crisp result.

\item {} 
If AlphaCut = True a genuine one-sided True-oriented cut is operated.

\item {} 
If StrictCut = True, the cut level value is excluded resulting in an open polarised valuation domain.
By default the polarised valuation domain is closed and the complementary indeterminate domain is open.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.flatten}\pysiglinewithargsret{\code{digraphs.}\bfcode{flatten}}{\emph{iterable}, \emph{ltypes=\textless{}class `collections.abc.Iterable'\textgreater{}}}{}
Flattens a list of lists into a flat list.

Main usage:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{listOfLists} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{4}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{n}{x} \PYG{k}{for} \PYG{n}{x} \PYG{o+ow}{in} \PYG{n}{flatten}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{listOfLists}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{]}
\PYG{g+go}{[1,2,3,4]}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{total\_size() (in module digraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:digraphs.total_size}\pysiglinewithargsret{\code{digraphs.}\bfcode{total\_size}}{\emph{o}, \emph{handlers=\{\}}, \emph{verbose=False}}{}
Returns the approximate memory footprint of an object and all of its contents.

Automatically finds the contents of the following containers and
their subclasses:  tuple, list, deque, dict, set, frozenset, Digraph and BigDigraph.
To search other containers, add handlers to iterate over their contents:
\begin{quote}
\begin{description}
\item[{handlers = \{SomeContainerClass: iter,}] \leavevmode
OtherContainerClass: OtherContainerClass.get\_elements\}

\end{description}
\end{quote}

See \href{http://code.activestate.com/recipes/577504/}{http://code.activestate.com/recipes/577504/}

\end{fulllineitems}


Back to the {\hyperref[techDoc:technical-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{Introduction}}}}


\subsection{graphs module}
\label{techDoc:graphs-label}\label{techDoc:graphs-module}
A tutorial with coding examples is available here: {\hyperref[tutorial:graphs-tutorial-label]{\emph{\DUspan{}{Working with the graphs module}}}}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:module-graphs}\index{graphs (module)}\index{BestDeterminedSpanningForest (class in graphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.BestDeterminedSpanningForest}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{graphs.}\bfcode{BestDeterminedSpanningForest}}{\emph{g}, \emph{seed=None}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Bases: {\hyperref[techDoc:graphs.RandomTree]{\emph{\code{graphs.RandomTree}}}}

Constructing the most determined spanning tree (or forest if not connected)
using Kruskal's greedy algorithm on the dual valuation.
\begin{description}
\item[{Example Python session:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RandomValuationGraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{seed}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showShort}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} short description of the graph \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Name             : \PYGZsq{}randomGraph\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Vertices         :  [\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v5\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{Valuation domain :  \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}med\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}0\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}min\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}\PYGZhy{}1\PYGZsq{}), \PYGZsq{}max\PYGZsq{}: Decimal(\PYGZsq{}1\PYGZsq{})\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Gamma function   : }
\PYG{g+go}{v1 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} [\PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{v2 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} [\PYGZsq{}v4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{v3 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} [\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{v4 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} [\PYGZsq{}v5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{v5 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} [\PYGZsq{}v4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{mt} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{BestDeterminedSpanningForest}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{mt}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{spanningTree}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{withSpanningTree}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} exporting a dot file for GraphViz tools \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Exporting to spanningTree.dot}
\PYG{g+go}{[[\PYGZsq{}v4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v5\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v4\PYGZsq{}]]}
\PYG{g+go}{neato \PYGZhy{}Tpng spanningTree.dot \PYGZhy{}o spanningTree.png}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

{\hfill\includegraphics{spanningTree.png}\hfill}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{DualGraph (class in graphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.DualGraph}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{graphs.}\bfcode{DualGraph}}{\emph{other}}{}
Bases: {\hyperref[techDoc:graphs.Graph]{\emph{\code{graphs.Graph}}}}

Instantiates the dual Graph object of a given other Graph instance.
\begin{description}
\item[{The relation constructor returns the dual of self.relation with formula:}] \leavevmode
relationOut{[}a{]}{[}b{]} = Max - self.relation{[}a{]}{[}b{]} + Min
where Max (resp. Min) equals valuation maximum (resp. minimum).

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\end{itemize}

\index{Graph (class in graphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.Graph}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{graphs.}\bfcode{Graph}}{\emph{fileName=None}, \emph{Empty=False}, \emph{numberOfVertices=7}, \emph{edgeProbability=0.5}}{}
Bases: \href{http://docs.python.org/library/functions.html\#object}{\code{object}}

In the \emph{graphs} module, the root {\hyperref[techDoc:graphs.Graph]{\emph{\code{graphs.Graph}}}} class provides a generic graph model. A given object consists in:
\begin{enumerate}
\item {} 
a vertices dictionary

\item {} 
a characteristic valuation domain, \{-1,0,+1\} by default

\item {} 
an edges dictionary, characterising each edge in the given valuation domain

\item {} 
a gamma function dictionary, holding the neighborhood vertices of each vertex

\end{enumerate}

General structure:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
vertices = \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: ...,\PYGZsq{}shortName\PYGZsq{}: ...\PYGZcb{},
            \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: ...,\PYGZsq{}shortName\PYGZsq{}: ...\PYGZcb{},
            \PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: ...,\PYGZsq{}shortName\PYGZsq{}: ...\PYGZcb{},
            ... \PYGZcb{}
valuationDomain = \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}min\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}1, \PYGZsq{}med\PYGZsq{}: 0, \PYGZsq{}max\PYGZsq{}: 1\PYGZcb{}
edges = \PYGZob{}frozenset(\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{},\PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}): 1,
         frozenset(\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{},\PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}): 1,
         frozenset(\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{},\PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}): \PYGZhy{}1,
           ...\PYGZcb{}
\PYGZsh{}\PYGZsh{} links from each vertex to its neighbors
gamma = \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{},v3\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}, \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}, \PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}, ... \PYGZcb{}
\end{Verbatim}
\begin{description}
\item[{Example python3 session:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{graphs} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{Graph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{Graph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{numberOfVertices}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{edgeProbability}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showShort}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} show short \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} short description of the graph \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Name             :  \PYGZsq{}random\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Vertices         :  [\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v2\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v4\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v5\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{Valuation domain :  \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}med\PYGZsq{}: 0, \PYGZsq{}max\PYGZsq{}: 1, \PYGZsq{}min\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZhy{}1\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{g+go}{Gamma function   :}
\PYG{g+go}{v1 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} [\PYGZsq{}v4\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{v2 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} []}
\PYG{g+go}{v3 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} [\PYGZsq{}v4\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{v4 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} [\PYGZsq{}v1\PYGZsq{}, \PYGZsq{}v3\PYGZsq{}]}
\PYG{g+go}{v5 \PYGZhy{}\PYGZgt{} []}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

\index{computeCliques() (graphs.Graph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.Graph.computeCliques}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeCliques}}{\emph{Comments=False}}{}
Computes all cliques, ie maximal complete subgraphs in self:

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
Computes the maximal independent vertex sets in the dual of self.

\item {} 
Result is stored in self.cliques.

\end{itemize}
\end{notice}

\end{fulllineitems}

\begin{fulllineitems}

Returns a partition of the vertices as a list

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeDiameter() (graphs.Graph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.Graph.computeDiameter}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeDiameter}}{\emph{Oriented=False}}{}
Renders the diameter (maximal neighbourhood depth) of the digraph instance.

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}
The diameter of a disconnected graph is considered to be \emph{infinite}
(results in a value -1) !
\end{notice}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{generateIndependent() (graphs.Graph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.Graph.generateIndependent}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{generateIndependent}}{\emph{U}}{}
Generator for all independent vertices sets
with neighborhoods of a graph instance:

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
Initiate with U = self.\_singletons().

\item {} 
Yields {[}independent set, covered set, all vertices - covered set){]}.

\item {} 
If independent set == (all vertices - covered set), the given independent set is maximal !

\end{itemize}
\end{notice}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{isTree() (graphs.Graph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.Graph.isTree}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{isTree}}{}{}

\index{randomDepthFirstSearch() (graphs.Graph method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.Graph.randomDepthFirstSearch}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{randomDepthFirstSearch}}{\emph{seed=None}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Depth first search through a graph in random order of the vertex keys.

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}
The resulting spanning tree (or forest) is by far not uniformly selected
among all possible trees. Spanning stars will indeed be much less
probably selected then streight walks !
\end{notice}

\end{fulllineitems}

to the parameters given.

\begin{notice}{note}{Note:}
Default values gives a normalized valuation domain
\end{notice}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{GridGraph (class in graphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.GridGraph}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{graphs.}\bfcode{GridGraph}}{\emph{n=5}, \emph{m=5}, \emph{valuationMin=-1}, \emph{valuationMax=1}}{}
Bases: {\hyperref[techDoc:graphs.Graph]{\emph{\code{graphs.Graph}}}}

Specialization of the general Graph class for generating
temporary Grid graphs of dimension n times m.
\begin{description}
\item[{\emph{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
n,m \textgreater{} 0

\item {} 
valuationDomain =\{`min':-1, `med':0, `max':+1\}

\end{itemize}

\item[{Default instantiation (5 times 5 Grid Digraph):}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
n = 5,

\item {} 
m=5,

\item {} 
valuationDomain = \{`min':-1.0,'max':1.0\}.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Example of 5x5 GridGraph instance:

{\hfill\includegraphics{grid-5-5.png}\hfill}

\index{IsingModel (class in graphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.IsingModel}\pysiglinewithargsret{\strong{class }\code{graphs.}\bfcode{IsingModel}}{\emph{g}, \emph{beta=0}, \emph{nSim=None}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Bases: {\hyperref[techDoc:graphs.Graph]{\emph{\code{graphs.Graph}}}}

Specialisation of a Gibbs Sampler for the Ising model
\begin{description}
\item[{Example:}] \leavevmode
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{GridGraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{n}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{15}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{m}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{15}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showShort}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} show short \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Grid graph    :  grid\PYGZhy{}6\PYGZhy{}6}
\PYG{g+go}{n             :  6}
\PYG{g+go}{m             :  6}
\PYG{g+go}{order         :  36}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{im} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{IsingModel}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{g}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{beta}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.3}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{nSim}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100000}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{Debug}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{False}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{Running a Gibbs Sampler for 100000 step !}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{im}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{colors}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{lightblue}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s}{lightcoral}\PYG{l+s}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} exporting a dot file for GraphViz tools \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{Exporting to grid\PYGZhy{}15\PYGZhy{}15\PYGZhy{}ising.dot}
\PYG{g+go}{fdp \PYGZhy{}Tpng grid\PYGZhy{}15\PYGZhy{}15\PYGZhy{}ising.dot \PYGZhy{}o grid\PYGZhy{}15\PYGZhy{}15\PYGZhy{}ising.png}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{description}

{\hfill\includegraphics{grid-15-15-ising.png}\hfill}
\index{computeSpinEnergy() (graphs.IsingModel method)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{techDoc:graphs.IsingModel.computeSpinEnergy}\pysiglinewithargsret{\bfcode{computeSpinEnergy}}{}{}
Spin energy H(c) of a spin configuration is
H(c) = -sum\_\{\{x,y\} in self.edges\}{[}spin\_c(x)*spin\_c(y){]}

\end{fulllineitems}
